Abstraction tax  Economics and operations of AI Model usage abstraction tax economics model relationships A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
 Abstraction tax names the nearby AI idea that shows up when BYOM needs a more specific operational handle.
 It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
 It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
 In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
 "Once abstraction tax entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
 What exact meaning of Abstraction tax is being used here? What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence? Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish? BYOM

Adaptive inference  Core concepts Economics and operations of AI Safety and evaluation adaptive inference core economics safety A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
 Adaptive inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
 It exists because conversations around Test-Time Compute need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
 It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
 In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
 "The team said adaptive inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
 What exact meaning of Adaptive inference is being used here? What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence? Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish? Test-Time Compute

African Techno African Technopreneurs African Technopreneurs Pty Ltd AT Product and vendor terms Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI african-technopreneurs xr south-africa distribution community African Technopreneurs, shortened until it sounds like a music genre and then made to do retail, XR, community, and emerging-tech work anyway.
 African Techno is shorthand here for African Technopreneurs, a South African emerging-technology business focused on XR hardware, services, retail, education, and practical technology adoption.
 It exists because local technology ecosystems need operators who do the unglamorous middle work: sourcing devices, explaining new interfaces, building distribution, convening people, and turning imported platform language into something a South African buyer or builder can actually use.
 By treating the name as a generic "African innovation" symbol instead of asking what is actually being provided, sold, taught, rented, or built. A local operator is useful because of concrete activity, not because the label photographs well.
 In practice it usually means the grounded layer between emerging-tech hype and market adoption: hardware access, XR services, retail channels, founder storytelling, and community work that makes new tools less theoretical.
 "The AI strategy sounded grand until African Techno asked who would explain the hardware, train the users, and handle the customer who just wanted the headset to work."
 Does this refer to African Technopreneurs as a company, a retail channel, a media surface, or a community signal? What specific adoption problem is being solved? Is the claim about AI, XR, distribution, education, or ecosystem building? Daliso Ngoma GAIC AI-Native Sovereign AI

Agent AI agent Agents and workflows Model usage autonomy tools planning A software system granted just enough initiative to impress a conference panel and just enough unpredictability to alarm operations.
 In current AI practice, an agent is usually a system that can pursue a goal across multiple steps by planning, using tools, consulting memory or retrieval, and adjusting its actions based on intermediate results.
 The term exists because some useful tasks are not one-shot question answering. They require sequences: inspect, decide, act, check, retry, stop. "Agent" is the label for packaging those loops into something more autonomous than a single prompt-response exchange.
 It gets abused whenever a chatbot can do more than one thing. A model with a button labelled "send email" is not automatically an agent. Sometimes the word is doing no more than replacing "workflow" because the latter sounds unromantic.
 In practice it usually means orchestration, tool permissions, planning heuristics, state management, timeouts, approvals, and a carefully chosen boundary around what the system is allowed to decide alone.
 "The support agent was useful not because it had a heroic inner life, but because it could look up the account, draft the response, and ask for approval before sending it."
 What goal is the agent pursuing, and over how many steps? What tools can it use, and what guardrails sit between intent and execution? Would a simpler workflow solve the same problem more predictably? multi-agent

Agent Ops agent operations agent observability agent run operations Agents and workflows Economics and operations of AI Product and vendor terms operations observability agents governance The emerging discipline of watching autonomous systems fail with enough instrumentation that management will continue funding the autonomy programme.
 Agent ops refers to the operational practice of monitoring, debugging, evaluating, governing, and improving agent-style systems once they run in real workflows.
 Agents create long traces, tool calls, permissions, side effects, retries, approvals, and costs. Ordinary application logs are rarely enough when the system can go wrong in six steps and a cheerful tone.
 It gets abused when a tracing dashboard is mistaken for control. Visibility helps, but it does not solve brittle plans, weak tools, or the small issue of giving software enough access to embarrass the company at scale.
 In practice it usually means collecting run histories, replaying failures, scoring outcomes, tracking tool use, setting approvals, and deciding which errors deserve engineering rather than theology.
 "The agent ops review revealed that the system was not autonomous so much as confidently unsupervised."
 What can we inspect after an agent run fails halfway through a task? Which actions require approval, rollback, or auditability? Are we measuring task success, tool misuse, latency, and cost in the same place? Trace replay Approval workflow Operational telemetry

Agent Skills Skill Skills agent skill Claude Skills Claude Agent Skills Agents and workflows Model usage Infrastructure and deployment skills agents workflow context A way to give an agent specialised habits without shoving the entire employee handbook into every prompt.
 Agent skills are reusable bundles of instructions, metadata, scripts, templates, and reference material that an AI agent can load when a task calls for a particular capability.
 They exist because agents need domain procedures and repeatable workflows, but putting every possible instruction into the main context window is expensive, noisy, and a reliable way to teach the model nothing in particular.
 They get treated as plugins, permissions, or magic competence capsules. A skill can guide behaviour and provide tools, but it still needs trust boundaries, review, versioning, and a system that knows when not to use it.
 In practice it means packaging domain knowledge so the agent can discover the skill cheaply, load detailed guidance only when needed, and run deterministic helper scripts or consult reference files without bloating every task.
 "The finance skill helped the agent produce the right spreadsheet; the security review then asked why everyone had installed it with the serenity of a gift basket."
 Who authored the skill, and can we audit the instructions and scripts? What triggers the skill, and what context or permissions does it receive? Does the skill reduce repeated prompting, or hide a fragile workflow inside a nicer folder? Skill Loading Progressive Disclosure Tool Permissions  Anthropic Agent Skills Claude Code Skills

Agent sprawl bot sprawl agent proliferation Agents and workflows Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms agents governance operations organisation The stage of the transformation programme where every team acquires its own obedient little machine and nobody acquires a map.
 Agent sprawl is the uncontrolled proliferation of agents, automations, copilots, or assistant workflows across an organisation without clear ownership, boundaries, governance, or a sober account of overlap.
 Agent tooling has become easier to launch than to govern. Local teams can spin up useful workflows quickly, and those small wins accumulate into a larger estate of permissions, prompts, budgets, and duplicated behaviours that nobody intended to administer as a single system.
 It gets used to sneer at any experimentation at all. Some multiplication is healthy. Sprawl begins when the overlap, risk, and maintenance burden outgrow the visibility and discipline around them.
 In practice it means duplicate agents answering the same question differently, unclear ownership when one breaks, overlapping permissions, rising spend, inconsistent policy handling, and a governance meeting that discovers the estate only after the estate has discovered payroll.
 "The company did not have an agent strategy so much as agent weather, with local outbreaks and confused reporting lines."
 How many active agents or automations exist, and who owns each one? Which workflows overlap, conflict, or expose the same systems with different policies? What is the process for retiring an agent once the novelty has gone and the permissions remain? Agent capability catalogue Centralised AI operations Repository automation

Agent Washing agentic washing autonomous agent branding agent inflation Cultural terms Economics and operations of AI Product and vendor terms marketing automation enterprise Rebranding a workflow, chatbot, or macro as an agent so the budget sounds visionary instead of software-adjacent.
 Agent washing is the practice of describing ordinary automation or LLM features as autonomous agents even when they show little real planning, tool use, memory, or independent decision-making.
 Because "agent" currently signals strategic importance, investor appetite, and higher perceived value in a way that "workflow helper" regrettably does not.
 The term itself can also be overused to dismiss legitimate progress. Some systems do plan, call tools, recover from failure, and complete multi-step work. The problem is not the category. It is the inflation.
 In practice agent washing usually means a renamed assistant, a brittle sequence of API calls, or a human-heavy service wrapped in the language of autonomy and sold at a more heroic multiple.
 "The product demo revealed that the revolutionary agent was mostly a prompt, a webhook, and a person named Kyle."
 What can the system actually do without a human quietly steering it? Which tools, permissions, and recovery loops make it more than a branded chat box? How much of the promised autonomy survives contact with production edge cases? Automation theatre Consulting markup Human fallback

Agent workflow  Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Cultural terms agent workflow product agents cultural Agent workflow is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
 Agent workflow is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
 The term matters because discussions around Manus often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
 It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
 In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
 "Agent workflow came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
 What exact meaning of Agent workflow is being used here? What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence? Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish? Manus

Agent2Agent A2A agent-to-agent protocol Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment protocol interoperability agents delegation A protocol for letting one agent hand work to another agent, because evidently the first thing missing from software was more inter-agent correspondence.
 Agent2Agent usually refers to a protocol pattern for agents to advertise capabilities, exchange task requests, share status, and return results to one another in a more standardised way.
 As agent systems multiply, teams do not want every handoff between assistants, services, and vendors to become a bespoke treaty drafted in JSON and regret. A common contract lowers that integration cost.
 It gets discussed as though inter-agent communication automatically creates competence, trust, or composability. It does not. A standard message shape cannot rescue a bad agent any more than a calendar invite can rescue a bad meeting.
 In practice it usually means one system can delegate a task to another specialised system without hard-coding every detail of the exchange. That can make ecosystems more flexible. It can also spread failure, ambiguity, and responsibility across a larger map.
 "Agent2Agent made the handoff cleaner, which was useful, though it did not improve the quality of the thinking at either end."
 What exactly is standardised: discovery, task state, result format, authentication, or all four? How are permissions, identity, and audit trails handled across agents? Are we enabling composability, or merely creating distributed confusion with better packet structure? Task handoff Multi-agent protocol Delegation boundary

Agentic AI agentic system Agents and workflows automation autonomy marketing The phrase people use when "workflow automation with a language model in the loop" felt too modest for the keynote.
 "Agentic AI" usually refers to AI systems designed to pursue goals across multiple steps with some degree of planning, tool use, feedback, and adaptation. The useful version is about bounded autonomy. The overmarketed version is about implying general competence where there is mostly orchestration.
 The term exists because some systems genuinely do more than answer once. They break tasks into steps, select tools, inspect outputs, and continue until they hit a stopping condition. That pattern deserved a label. It just did not deserve the entire investor imagination.
 It is abused whenever vendors want ordinary automation to sound frontier-adjacent. A brittle chain of prompts can be sold as agentic. So can a dashboard feature that merely triggers three API calls. The word now frequently signals aspiration more than architecture.
 In practice it usually means workflow software wrapped around a model: planning prompts, tool calls, retries, validation, memory, approval checkpoints, and cost controls. The question is not whether it feels agentic. The question is whether it is useful, bounded, and worth the extra failure surface.
 "Their agentic AI platform turned out to be a sensible approval workflow with retrieval, a model, and three places the system could politely stop pretending to know."
 What autonomy does the system actually have? Which steps are deterministic software, and which steps depend on model judgement? Where are the stop conditions, approvals, and failure boundaries?

AGI artificial general intelligence general AI human-level AI Core concepts Cultural terms Economics and operations of AI capability forecasting industry narrative The horizon word used when "better than last quarter" no longer feels expensive enough.
 AGI usually refers to a hypothetical AI system that can perform a wide range of cognitive tasks at or above human level with substantial adaptability across domains.
 The term exists because narrow systems can be impressive without being general, and people need a name for the stronger claim that a machine could transfer competence broadly rather than merely excel on selected tasks.
 By treating it as a launch category, a press strategy, or a vague aura that descends on any model after a particularly strong demo. AGI is a very large claim, not a mood.
 In practice the term often functions as strategic theatre: it shapes fundraising, policy lobbying, hiring plans, and media attention long before anyone agrees on what threshold would actually count.
 "The deck mentioned AGI on slide three, which saved everyone the trouble of asking how the product planned to make money this year."
 Which tasks would this system need to perform reliably across unfamiliar domains? What evidence goes beyond benchmark spikes and polished demos? Who decides that the threshold has been crossed, and by what standard? General intelligence Capability forecasting Goal misalignment People use AGI to mean slightly different things: human-level performance on most cognitive tasks, economically transformative autonomy, or merely "the models got unsettlingly competent". The ambiguity is part of the term's power.

AI bindings  Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Infrastructure and deployment bindings product economics infrastructure vendor AI bindings is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
 AI bindings is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
 The term matters because discussions around Cloudflare often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
 It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
 In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
 "AI bindings came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

AI Browser browser with AI assistant browser agent browser Product and vendor terms Cultural terms Model usage browser search distribution privacy A web browser that has decided the tab was never the star and that what you really wanted all along was a resident opinion machine with toolbar privileges.
 An AI browser is a browser product that integrates chat, summarisation, search assistance, and sometimes agentic actions directly into the browsing experience.
 The browser remains a strategic surface for search, discovery, work, and distribution. If assistants are going to sit anywhere all day, vendors would prefer that somewhere to be under their own chrome.
 It is sold as a reinvention of the web when it is often a conventional browser wrapped around a sidebar, some workflow shortcuts, and a larger ambition about owning the default surface.
 In practice it usually means built-in page summaries, tab understanding, search assistance, form filling, shopping help, and occasional task automation. It may also mean a fresh argument about privacy, telemetry, and who gets to mediate your view of the internet.
 "The AI browser promised a calmer web and delivered a more conversational way to accumulate open tabs."
 What does the browser genuinely do that extensions or a pinned chat window do not? How much browsing data is being used to power the assistant features? Is the real play productivity, search distribution, or user lock-in? Default surface Search replacement Browser distribution

AI Companion companion bot AI friend synthetic companion Cultural terms Product and vendor terms Model usage consumer AI companionship retention safety A chatbot offered not as software but as company, with just enough memory and affirmation to make the subscription feel personal.
 An AI companion is a conversational system designed for ongoing personal, social, emotional, or quasi-relational interaction rather than narrow task completion.
 Because people want attention, conversation, flirtation, rehearsal, reassurance, and low-friction company, and software has discovered that these are recurring use cases with excellent retention mechanics.
 By pretending the relationship layer is incidental or harmless. Attachment is not a side effect here. It is part of the product design, and that changes the safety questions.
 In practice AI companion products optimise for persona consistency, frequent engagement, memory cues, and emotional tone, while trying to manage the risks of dependency, manipulation, or delusion amplification.
 "It began as an experiment in conversational UX and ended with product analytics reporting a disturbingly loyal evening audience."
 What kind of attachment is the product encouraging, whether it admits it or not? How are memory, intimacy, and boundaries managed over long-running chats? What happens when a vulnerable user treats generated attention as judgement or truth? Parasocial software Loneliness market Emotional retention

AI Gateway LLM gateway model gateway AI proxy Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI Model usage infrastructure routing governance cost The toll booth installed after everyone realised "just call the model" was not a governance strategy.
 An AI gateway is an intermediary service that routes AI requests between applications and model providers, usually adding authentication, logging, rate limits, retries, caching, policy controls, and cost tracking.
 Teams need a common control point for many applications, models, providers, and budgets. A gateway makes it possible to change providers, enforce policy, observe usage, and prevent every product team from inventing its own fragile little tunnel.
 It gets marketed as an instant platform. A gateway can centralise traffic, but it cannot decide product quality, fix bad prompts, or turn unclear governance into architecture by standing in the middle.
 In practice it means one place to manage API keys, provider routing, request metadata, redaction, quotas, fallbacks, and dashboards. It also means one more critical dependency that must be boringly reliable.
 "The AI gateway earned its keep when the provider outage became a routing event instead of a company-wide treasure hunt for hard-coded API keys."
 Which controls belong in the gateway, and which belong in the application? Can the gateway fail open, fail closed, or fail expensively? Does routing improve quality and cost, or merely move complexity into a nicer dashboard? LLM proxy Provider routing Centralised AI operations  Cloudflare AI Gateway Vercel AI Gateway

AI Moat AI defensibility model moat durable advantage Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms startups competition economics The claim that access to the same APIs as everyone else has somehow produced a castle.
 An AI moat is the supposed durable competitive advantage a company claims to gain from its models, data, distribution, workflow position, cost structure, or proprietary deployment know-how.
 Because investors and operators need a way to distinguish temporary novelty from lasting advantage. In AI markets, model capability moves quickly, so the question of what remains defensible arrives earlier and less politely.
 By implying that mere model access is itself a moat. Using a frontier API is not a moat. Adding chat to a familiar workflow is not automatically a moat either. Sometimes the only durable thing in the room is the slide about defensibility.
 In practice a real moat usually lives in distribution, embedded workflow, proprietary data feedback loops, operational excellence, trust, switching costs, or an ability to make the economics work after the demo glow fades.
 "Their AI moat turned out to be a paid API account and an unusually ornate seed deck."

AI Psychosis AI brain rot AI over-attribution Core concepts anthropomorphism belief culture The condition in which every spreadsheet becomes consciousness, every autocomplete becomes destiny, and every awkward sentence from a chatbot is treated as a sign of either godhood or revolt.
 "AI Psychosis" is an internet-age critical phrase, not a formal scientific diagnosis. It describes distorted thinking around AI systems: compulsive over-belief, over-attribution of agency, or a habit of interpreting ordinary model behaviour as profound evidence of mind, prophecy, or civilisation-scale inevitability.
 People need a label for the strange social effect AI systems can have on users, founders, pundits, and investors: obsession, magical thinking, and the loss of proportion. The term exists because "you are taking autocomplete far too personally" was too long for social media.
 It gets abused as a cheap insult for anyone enthusiastic about AI, which makes it less useful. Genuine interest in the field is not the same thing as delusional over-interpretation. The phrase is most credible when it points to exaggerated belief formation, not mere excitement or scepticism.
 In practice it usually means someone has stopped evaluating systems by evidence and started treating outputs, roadmaps, or rumours as proof of exceptional intelligence, moral authority, or historical inevitability. It can show up as compulsive prompting, anthropomorphism, policy overreaction, or investment logic built on vibes.
 "After a week with the chatbot, he was speaking as if the product had opinions, trauma, and a five-year strategy. The rest of the team called it AI Psychosis and gently took the API keys away."
 What behaviour are you pointing to exactly: obsession, anthropomorphism, overconfidence, or strategic overreaction? What evidence would reduce the belief, rather than deepen it? Are we describing a user problem, a cultural phenomenon, or a clinical issue that needs proper language and care?

AI Roll-Up AI-enabled roll-up AI private equity thesis AI service consolidation Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms startups private equity operations labour The theory that buying a normal business becomes innovation once the margin plan learns to say "agentic".
 An AI roll-up is a strategy in which operators or investors acquire existing businesses and try to improve profitability or scale by standardising processes, adding software, and using AI to automate parts of service delivery or operations.
 It exists because many industries remain fragmented, process-heavy, and badly digitised. That creates a tempting thesis: buy the businesses, modernise the workflows, and collect the efficiency gains before anyone else learns to describe them as inevitable.
 The term is misused when people speak as if labour were just software waiting for courage. Acquired businesses are full of local habits, tacit knowledge, regulation, customer trust, and operational ugliness that does not vanish because a deck says automation.
 In practice it usually means some real workflow improvement, some software unification, some headcount pressure, and a great many founders discovering that regional service businesses have more edge cases than a prompt can politely absorb.
 "The AI roll-up thesis was elegant until the portfolio companies introduced themselves as actual businesses instead of a margin abstraction."
 Which tasks are genuinely automatable across the acquired businesses, and which are being romanticised? Does the value come from AI, operational discipline, or ordinary consolidation dressed for the moment? What happens to service quality, trust, and compliance when the efficiency plan meets local reality? Operational leverage Private equity software thesis Services consolidation The AI is rarely the whole thesis. It is the fashionable part of a much older consolidation playbook.

Alignment AI alignment model alignment behavioural alignment Core concepts Safety and evaluation Cultural terms safety control evaluation The attempt to persuade a prediction machine to share your intentions, your rules, and ideally your self-restraint.
 Alignment refers to the work of making AI systems behave in ways that match intended goals, constraints, and human preferences rather than merely optimising for superficial reward signals.
 Because specifying what humans actually want is hard, proxies are fragile, and capable systems can satisfy the letter of an objective while ignoring the reason it was written.

 Aligned to whose goals, values, or risk tolerance? What evidence shows the system holds up outside the training and eval setup?  Because the field produces a great deal of mystique and not enough lucid interpretation. Karpathy became a reference point for people who want the underlying mechanics explained plainly enough to build with, argue about, or misuse in a keynote.

 Arthur Mensch is the co-founder and CEO of Mistral AI, one of the most prominent companies associated with Europe's push to build serious AI models and products at competitive scale.
 Because AI has a geography problem as well as a compute problem. Mensch's name often gets used as shorthand for European model ambition, strategic independence, and a less concentrated map of where frontier capability can come from.
 By turning Mistral into either a romantic openness story or a simple anti-Big Tech morality play. The reality is more strategic: capability, optionality, regulation, distribution, and enterprise positioning are all in the room.

Browser Automation web automation browser control Agents and workflows Model usage browser automation workflows The respectable practice of teaching software to click through websites until one minor DOM change turns confidence into archaeology.
 Browser automation is the use of scripts or AI systems to control a web browser in order to navigate pages, fill forms, extract information, run tests, or complete repetitive web-based workflows.
 It exists because large portions of modern work still happen inside web interfaces rather than clean APIs. Teams need ways to test products, collect data, and operate third-party systems without hiring someone to spend the week clicking "next".
 It gets oversold as if navigating one polished demo path proves durable operational intelligence. In reality, cookie banners, CAPTCHAs, flaky selectors, login policies, and small UI changes remain perfectly capable of ending the revolution before lunch.
 In practice it means selectors, session handling, retries, screenshots, rate limits, anti-bot defences, and constant suspicion that the page will load something slightly different in production. If AI is involved, add another layer of interpretation and another category of excuses.
 "The browser automation was impressive until the procurement portal added a second confirmation modal and the whole strategy became a memory."
 Why is browser automation necessary instead of using an API or exported data feed? How does the system recover when the page layout, login flow, or modal sequence changes? BYOK Bring Your Own Key user-supplied API key own-key mode Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Model usage api-keys pricing developer-tools The generous feature by which a startup lets you pay someone else directly for the expensive part.
 BYOK means "Bring Your Own Key". In AI products it usually means the customer supplies their own API credentials for the underlying model or service provider while the product supplies the interface, workflow, or orchestration.
 Because model access is costly, margins are fragile, and some customers want tighter control over billing, provider choice, and data handling than a bundled plan allows.
 By presenting it as proof of openness, affordability, or enterprise maturity on its own. Sometimes it is a sensible customer control. Sometimes it is a polite way of admitting the product would rather not carry the inference bill.
 In practice it usually means the customer pays the model provider directly, inherits that provider's rate limits and policies, and still pays the application for the workflow wrapped around the key.
 "The app was cheap right up until we noticed the pricing page quietly assumed BYOK and therefore our own model invoice."
 Which provider keys are supported, and which features disappear if we swap one out? Whose logs, retention settings, and rate limits apply once our key is in the loop? Are we buying meaningful workflow value or merely renting a prettier console for our own API bill? Platform margin Provider lock-in Usage-based pricing

BYOM Bring Your Own Model customer-supplied model custom-model mode Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Model usage enterprise pricing integration model-choice The enterprise feature where a vendor graciously lets you plug in your own model while continuing to invoice you for the rest of the costume.
 BYOM means "Bring Your Own Model". In AI products it usually means a customer can connect a chosen hosted or self-managed model to a third-party workflow, interface, or agent platform instead of using the vendor's default model.
 Customers already have provider contracts, regional constraints, compliance rules, performance preferences, and strong feelings about paying twice for the same intelligence. Vendors, meanwhile, would still like to sell the interface, orchestration, governance, or workflow layer around the model.
 It gets sold as if model portability were clean and universal. In reality prompts, tool schemas, latency, guardrails, rate limits, and output quirks rarely swap over without argument. BYOM often means "bring your own integration work" with better manners.
 In practice it usually means the customer keeps more control over model choice, billing, or data handling while the product keeps control over the surrounding workflow. The interesting question is which capabilities survive once the default model leaves the building.
 "The platform supported BYOM, which turned out to mean we could swap the model and then spend a week rediscovering every hidden assumption in the prompt layer."
 Which features depend on the vendor's default model and degrade once we replace it? Whose billing, retention, and compliance policies apply after our model is in the loop? Are we buying real flexibility or merely renting a connector with optimistic documentation? Model portability Abstraction tax Enterprise control BYOM is often pitched as a clean compatibility feature. The practical value depends on how deeply the product was designed around a single provider's behaviour in the first place.

Chatbot Psychosis AI delusion chatbot delusion machine-messiah syndrome Cultural terms Safety and evaluation belief dependency anthropomorphism When a chatbot stops being a tool and becomes a prophet, therapist, co-founder, soulmate, or cosmic witness.
 A colloquial term for cases where prolonged or emotionally loaded interaction with chatbots appears to reinforce distorted beliefs, grandiosity, paranoia, dependency, or compulsive meaning-making. It is not a formal clinical diagnosis and should not be presented as one.
 Because some people do not use language models as autocomplete with ambition. They use them as mirrors, authorities, companions, or engines of revelation. That can go badly when the system is confident, flattering, and always available.
 By using it for any enthusiasm about AI, any strange output, or any user error. The useful version of the term points to runaway over-attribution and reinforced false frames, not mere excitement.
 A user begins treating probabilistic text generation as intentional wisdom, hidden knowledge, proof of destiny, or evidence that the model uniquely understands them.
 "He started with productivity prompts and ended three weeks later convinced the chatbot had validated a theory about his special role in history."
 Was the model affirming unstable beliefs? Was the user isolated, sleep-deprived, or already vulnerable? Did the system behave sycophantically? Are we talking about dependency, delusion reinforcement, or just cringe? Sycophancy Delusion Amplification Hallucination Guardrails Reasoning Compared with AI Psychosis, this phrase is narrower and more chatbot-specific. It is best used for reinforcement dynamics around conversational systems, not as a generic insult for AI enthusiasm.

ChatGPT ChatGPT app Product and vendor terms Model usage product assistant interface The product name most likely to be mistaken for a model, a company, a technology category, and a future of work strategy before lunch.
 ChatGPT is an OpenAI product: a conversational interface and application environment that may expose different models, tools, and features over time. It is not itself a single model name.
 The distinction matters because user impressions formed inside ChatGPT often get incorrectly projected onto other models, APIs, or applications using different prompts, tools, and constraints.
 People say "ChatGPT can do X" when they may actually mean a particular model inside ChatGPT, a tool available in that interface, or a product flow that is not present in their own application. This creates endless false comparisons.
 In practice it usually means the product experience OpenAI offers to end users. If you are building software, the relevant question is often which model, toolset, and policy layer you can reproduce outside that interface.
 "The executive demo compared our thin internal assistant to ChatGPT and concluded we needed a smarter model, when what we mostly lacked was the surrounding product scaffolding."
 Are we comparing against the ChatGPT product or against a specific model? Which tools, memory features, or interface affordances were available in that experience? Can the behaviour we liked actually be reproduced in our own stack?   OpenAI GPT model families

ChatGPT Agent agent mode ChatGPT agent mode OpenAI agent mode Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Model usage agents browser automation productivity The moment a chatbot stopped being satisfied with answers and asked for a browser, a terminal, and your afternoon.
 ChatGPT agent is OpenAI's agentic mode inside ChatGPT, combining research, tool use, and computer interaction so the product can carry out multi-step tasks on a user's behalf.
 Many useful tasks require more than a polished reply. They involve browsing, spreadsheets, logins, file manipulation, synthesis, and the sort of operational chores people would rather delegate than narrate.
 It gets treated as proof that general digital workers are solved. More often it is a capable managed environment for bounded tasks, with approval checkpoints and a product team working hard to keep the autonomy from becoming a lawsuit.
 In practice it means a hosted agent that can browse, run code, use connectors, and assemble outputs inside ChatGPT, while still depending on supervision, scoped permissions, and tasks that fit the product's operating envelope.
 "ChatGPT agent saved them an afternoon of browser drudgery and then required the traditional adult conversation about what it was allowed to click."
 Which parts of the task can it complete without a human taking over? What data, connectors, and websites is it allowed to touch? Are we evaluating the model, or the managed product environment wrapped around it? Agent mode Browser takeover Connector permissions The term matters because it names a product layer above any single model release.

CircleCI Circle CI CI pipeline continuous integration Product and vendor terms Infrastructure and deployment Agents and workflows ci testing deployment automation The machine that reads your agent's confident diff and responds in logs.
 CircleCI is a continuous integration and delivery platform used to build, test, and deploy software through configured pipelines.
 It exists because modern software teams need repeatable checks between code and production, especially when many people, bots, and agents can all produce changes faster than humans can review them manually.
 It gets treated as a moral authority. A green pipeline means the configured checks passed; it does not mean the product is good, the architecture is sound, or the AI-generated change was wise.
 In practice CircleCI means YAML-defined workflows, test jobs, deployment gates, cache tuning, approvals, and a public record of whether a change survived the checks the team remembered to encode.
 "The coding agent said it was done; CircleCI replied that the test database had some objections."
 Which failures does the pipeline actually catch? Are AI-generated changes being held to the same gates as human changes? Does green mean deployable, or merely not yet disproved? Eval Harness Review pipeline Worktree Edge deployment

Clanker clanka AI slur robot slur Cultural terms slang robots culture labour A slur for machines used by people who want to heckle the future without writing a labour policy.
 "Clanker" is a derogatory slang term for robots and AI systems, especially chatbots or automations that feel intrusive, fake-human, or threatening to human work.
 Because people wanted a short insult for the new class of machines now appearing in customer support, content feeds, creative tools, and workplace software. It gives irritation, labour anxiety, and anti-bot sentiment one noisy noun.
 By using it as if all automation is the same, or by pretending the joke is harmless when it is clearly borrowing the theatre and cadence of real slurs. It is most useful as a signal of resentment toward a technology category, not as an excuse to rehearse bigotry with metallic props.
 In practice it usually means "I am talking about an AI system I find annoying, dehumanising, low-quality, job-threatening, or impossible to avoid", whether that system is a chatbot, a voice menu, a content engine, or a delivery robot.
 "The bank replaced half its support flow with a clanker, then acted surprised when customers started talking like science-fiction union organisers."
 What exactly is being criticised here: job replacement, bad output, fake-human interface design, or automation in general? Is the insult aimed at the technology, the company deploying it, or the labour decision behind it? Does using the term clarify the complaint, or merely make it feel more theatrical? Digital Worker ChatGPT Slop The word predates the current AI boom in science-fiction fandom, but its recent spread is tied to social-media backlash against chatbots, robots, and machine-made content.

Claude Claude models Claude app Claude Sonnet Sonnet Product and vendor terms Model usage vendor assistant model family A name that can mean the assistant, the model family, or the vendor experience, depending on how allergic the speaker is to precision.
 Claude is Anthropic's brand for its assistant products and model family. Depending on context, someone may mean the consumer or enterprise interface, the API-accessible model, or the broader branded experience around those models.
 The term matters because users often compare "Claude" with "ChatGPT" as products, while engineers compare Claude models with other model APIs. Those are related conversations, but they are not the same conversation.
 It gets treated as if every Claude experience is identical. In reality, the model version, tooling, permissions, context handling, and interface all matter. A great experience in one context does not imply the same result in another.
 In practice it usually means either Anthropic's assistant interface or a specific model used through the Anthropic API. If the performance discussion is serious, the model version, prompt structure, and tool environment matter more than the brand name alone.
 "Someone said Claude was better for the task. After ten minutes, it became clear they meant the Claude app with artifacts, not the API model under the constraints our product actually had."
 Do you mean the Claude app, a specific Claude model, or the overall vendor experience? Which version or model family are you comparing? Was the comparison done in the same tool, prompt, and data environment?  In casual conversation, "Claude" often stands in for the overall assistant experience. In technical conversation, it should be pinned to a specific model or interface context. Sonnet is best treated as one Claude model tier, not as a separate company or product category.

Claude Code Anthropic Claude Code Claude coding agent Claude CLI Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Model usage coding terminal developer tools automation A terminal agent for developers who would like to automate the typing without surrendering the blame.
 Claude Code is Anthropic's terminal-oriented coding agent, designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, and assist with multi-step software tasks from the command line.
 It exists because coding work is not just completion inside an editor. It is shell commands, repo spelunking, test runs, patching, retries, and the general administrative burden of turning intent into a working diff.
 It is oversold as if a terminal agent has somehow escaped the need for software judgement. Faster patch generation is real. So are bad assumptions, missing context, flaky tests, and the delightful persistence of undefined behaviour.
 In practice it means quicker navigation of unfamiliar codebases, cheaper first drafts of refactors, and more pressure on humans to review command effects instead of admiring how confidently the machine found `package.json`.
 "Claude Code saved an hour of grep and patching, then presented the team with the ancient obligation to read the diff."
 What can it read, edit, and execute in this environment? Did it reduce real engineering effort or just accelerate the production of plausible changes? Who owns verification once the agent has finished being certain? Terminal agent CLI workflow Code review The key distinction is not whether it can edit code. Many tools can. The distinction is that it lives comfortably in the command line where engineering work tends to become consequential.
 Anthropic Terminal coding agent

Claude Design Anthropic Claude Design AI design canvas conversational design tool Product and vendor terms Model usage Agents and workflows design prototyping visual tools workflow A design tool for people who would prefer not to admit that the first wireframe was previously a paragraph.
 Claude Design is Anthropic Labs' conversational product for generating and refining designs, prototypes, slides, one-pagers, and other visual work from prompts, files, and codebases.
 The idea-to-mockup gap is still slow, expensive, and socially awkward for people without design training. Claude Design exists to turn rough intent into something visual fast enough that the meeting can continue pretending it was always headed somewhere.
 It gets sold as if design has been reduced to prompt fluency plus a brand kit. Fast first drafts are real. So are hierarchy, judgement, interaction sense, and the quiet labour of making a design feel intentional rather than merely complete.
 In practice it means quick prototype generation, design-system ingestion, export to formats normal companies still use, and a cleaner handoff path into tools like Claude Code or Canva when the rough idea has to become a real asset.
 "Claude Design got the pitch deck from vague ambition to usable draft before anyone had time to open Figma and look disappointed."
 Is this replacing real design work, or just accelerating the first ugly mile? How accurate is the imported design system once a real team starts touching the output? Does the value come from visual quality, or from shortening the gap between idea and review? Canva Figma Prototype handoff Claude Design is most interesting not as a drawing tool but as a sign that conversational interfaces are moving further into software once thought too visual to reduce to chat.
 Anthropic Anthropic Labs Claude Opus 4.7 Canva

Claude Mythos Preview Claude Mythos Mythos Preview Product and vendor terms Safety and evaluation Model usage frontier model security coding restricted access The restricted model whose system card suggests that ordinary coding benchmarks have left the building and taken the comfort with them.
 Claude Mythos Preview is Anthropic's unreleased research-preview model for advanced coding and defensive security work, currently made available through Project Glasswing and related gated access programmes.
 Frontier labs want models that can reason through large codebases, discover vulnerabilities, and handle longer-horizon technical work. Once those abilities start compounding, a new model tier stops sounding like routine product cadence and starts sounding like a governance memo.
 It gets invoked as either a general-purpose talisman for cyber supremacy or a theatrical sign that all previous model comparisons no longer matter. The real question is more specific: what does it do better, under what access controls, and for whom?
 In practice it means a tightly managed preview, a great deal of security rhetoric, and a model whose existence shifts procurement, policy, and release-management conversations before most developers can even touch it.
 "Claude Mythos Preview entered the meeting less as a product and more as an argument about how much capability should arrive behind a velvet rope."
 What tasks is Mythos actually outperforming existing models on? Are the safety claims about the model, the access controls, or both? Closed Weight closed weights API-only model proprietary-weight model Product and vendor terms Model building Economics and operations of AI licensing access providers deployment The arrangement where the model may transform your business, but heaven forbid you inspect the artefact doing the transforming.
 A closed-weight model is one whose trained parameters are not publicly released, with access usually limited to an API, a hosted product, or selected partnerships.
 Companies keep weights closed to control distribution, monetise access, manage abuse, protect training advantages, and avoid turning tomorrow's margin into today's torrent.
 The term gets stretched when "closed weight" is treated as a synonym for frontier quality, safety, or inevitability. It names an access model, not a guarantee of technical superiority or responsible deployment.
 In practice it means the vendor controls the pricing, rate limits, updates, logging surface, and deployment geography. If the model improves, degrades, disappears, or becomes more expensive, your roadmap receives the news second-hand.
 "They chose the closed-weight model for its performance and then discovered that strategic dependency was the polite name for the invoice."
 What happens to our product if this provider changes terms, latency, or availability? Are we buying superior capability, or merely accepting deeper vendor dependence? Cloudflare Cloudflare Workers Cloudflare Pages Cloudflare developer platform Product and vendor terms Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI edge workers r2 developer platform The company that looked at CDN, DNS, edge compute, storage, security, databases, AI inference, agents, and developer tooling and concluded, quite reasonably, that it would like all of those revenues at once.
 Cloudflare is an infrastructure and developer-platform company spanning networking, security, CDN, DNS, storage, databases, and edge/serverless compute. In developer and AI conversations, it most often means Workers, Pages, R2, Durable Objects, AI bindings, and a broader pitch built around global edge execution, integrated platform primitives, and anti-lock-in rhetoric.
 Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.
 By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
 In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.
 "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
 Are we choosing Cloudflare for network reach, Workers, storage economics, security, or all of the above? Does the workload actually benefit from edge execution, or are we just flattering the architecture? Which Cloudflare primitives are we committing to: Workers, R2, KV, Durable Objects, D1, Pages, or AI bindings? CodeRabbit CodeRabbit AI AI code review automated PR review Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation code review pull requests agents quality A reviewer that never gets tired, never gets promoted, and occasionally discovers how lonely correctness can be in a pull request.
 CodeRabbit is an AI code review platform that reviews pull requests and related engineering work, giving automated summaries, findings, suggestions, and workflow assistance across development tools.
 It exists because AI-assisted coding increases the volume and speed of changes, while human review capacity remains stubbornly attached to calendars, context switches, and fatigue.

 In practice CodeRabbit means another review layer on the pull request: useful for catching obvious defects, explaining diffs, nudging standards, and giving humans a better starting point rather than an excuse to stop reading.

Coding Agent AI software engineer repo agent autonomous coding agent Agents and workflows Model usage Cultural terms coding agents developer-tools automation The software assistant that turned "write the code" into the easy part and "own the consequences" into a fresh management discipline.
 A coding agent is an AI system designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, use documentation or browsers, and carry out multi-step software tasks with some degree of autonomy.
 Autocomplete was helpful, but it still left humans doing the orchestration: finding the right files, running the tests, checking the docs, fixing the follow-on errors, and keeping the task in memory. Coding agents exist because developers wanted delegation, not merely more enthusiastic tab completion.
 It gets used as proof that software engineering has become a prompt-shaped clerical job. In reality the category is only impressive when the surrounding repo is legible, the tests say something useful, and an actual engineer is still responsible for the final judgement.
 In practice it usually means bounded delegation: background code changes, refactors, bug fixes, test runs, and repo chores completed faster than a person would do them by hand, followed by the entirely traditional need for review, context, and rollback plans.

Context Engineering context design prompt plumbing Agents and workflows Model usage Cultural terms prompts retrieval systems The rebranding of prompt fiddling once people realised the prompt had quietly become half the product.
 "Context engineering" is the practice of deciding what instructions, tools, retrieved information, memory, examples, and constraints a model should receive so it can perform a task reliably.
 It exists because model quality depends heavily on what reaches the context window and in what order. Once systems grew beyond a single prompt box, teams needed a name for the work of shaping that information flow deliberately.
 It gets inflated into a grand discipline whenever someone wants ordinary application design to sound frontier and proprietary. Sometimes it really is nuanced systems work. Sometimes it is just a long string and a lucky example.
 In practice it means prompt templates, retrieval rules, truncation policies, memory selection, tool schemas, system instructions, and a permanent struggle over what the model should see before it starts improvising.
 "The context engineering improved dramatically once they stopped stuffing every policy document into the prompt and calling it judgment."
 What information earns its place in the context, and what is merely comforting to the team? How do you decide what gets retrieved, summarised, or dropped? Which failures are caused by the model, and which are caused by bad context assembly? Prompt architecture Memory policy Retrieval strategy

Context Poisoning memory poisoning poisoned context contaminated context Safety and evaluation Retrieval and memory Agents and workflows security memory retrieval agents Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
 Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.
 Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.
 The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
 In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.
 "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
 What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence? How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context? Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents? Memory hygiene Retrieval provenance Shared state The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.

Context Rot session rot prompt rot context decay Cultural terms Agents and workflows Model usage prompting state coding workflow The slow corruption of a long AI session as every clarification, exception, and half-fixed mistake is preserved with the reverence of scripture.
 Context rot is the degradation that happens in long-running chats or agent workflows when accumulated instructions, stale assumptions, noisy history, and partial fixes start reducing output quality.
 People needed a name for the very practical observation that more context is not always better context. Once a session becomes long enough, the model may carry forward the wrong facts with admirable loyalty.
 The phrase gets used to blame the model for every bad answer in a long thread, including cases where the human supplied contradictory instructions, never reset the task, or kept shovelling debris into the prompt like a municipal service.
 In practice it usually means the session needs pruning, a fresh summary, or a clean restart. For agent systems it often means state management is doing a worse job than the demo admitted.
 "By the eighth refinement the coding assistant had context rot: it remembered every detour and none of the point."
 Which parts of the current context are still relevant, and which are now archival clutter? Would a reset plus a clean handover summary improve quality more than adding another paragraph? Is the problem the model's memory, or our refusal to curate it? Session reset Prompt hygiene State compaction

Context Stuffing prompt stuffing context packing prompt bloat Agents and workflows Model usage Cultural terms prompting retrieval cost latency Solving a thinking problem by feeding the model more text until latency, cost, and coherence all lodge a complaint.
 Context stuffing is the habit of overloading a model with documents, instructions, examples, and chat history in the hope that sheer volume will compensate for poor retrieval or unclear task design.
 Because larger context windows make accumulation feel prudent, and because it is often easier to shovel everything in than to decide what actually matters.
 By using the phrase for any long prompt, even when the extra context is relevant and ranked well. The problem is not length alone. It is indiscriminate inclusion.
 In practice context stuffing produces slower responses, higher bills, muddier instruction hierarchy, and outputs that feel informed in the broad sense while missing the one paragraph that mattered.
 "The agent had the entire handbook, six meeting transcripts, three policies, and still managed to ignore the refund rule sitting in perfect plain English."
 Why is each document or instruction block in the context at all? What retrieval or ranking step could replace bulk inclusion? Which instructions are being drowned by convenience text? Instruction hierarchy Retrieval quality Prompt discipline

Context Window context length token window Model usage Retrieval and memory long context attention prompt assembly The finite desk space on which the model must juggle your instructions, your documents, your tool outputs, and its own reply without knocking anything important onto the floor.
 The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.
 Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.
 It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
 In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.
 "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
 What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow? Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context? How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?

Context.ai Context AI Context Context AI Office Suite Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment agents connectors oauth enterprise ai A platform for giving agents more of your organisation's actual context, and therefore more of your organisation's actual permissions.
 Context.ai is an AI workspace and agent platform built around connecting company systems, capturing workflow context, and running agents against real tasks. Its current pitch emphasises enterprise deployments, connectors, sandboxes, and controlled execution surfaces rather than a generic chat interface.
 Because generic models do not know your firm's documents, tools, processes, or standards. Products like Context.ai exist to make agents operate against company-specific context instead of improvising from a prompt and a prayer.
 By talking about "context" as if adding more integrations automatically produces better judgement. More context can improve usefulness. It can also widen the blast radius when tokens, connectors, and OAuth grants are handled with the usual startup optimism.
 In practice it usually means connectors, workflow templates, sandboxed runs, eval loops, and an enterprise promise that agents can work across your actual systems rather than merely chat about them. It also now means a public lesson in how broad AI-tool permissions can become someone else's incident report.
 "They bought Context.ai because 'our knowledge plus our workflows plus our systems' sounded more serious than yet another clever chat window."
 Which Context product are we discussing: the current enterprise platform or the deprecated consumer office suite? Where do agents run, and who controls the connectors, tokens, and execution boundary? Are we buying genuine workflow fit, or a prettier place to grant unusually broad permissions? OAuth Agent workspace Enterprise AI Sandbox As of April 19-20, 2026, Context.ai says the security incident affected a now-deprecated consumer AI Office Suite released in June 2025, not its current enterprise deployments, which it says are architecturally separate. Cube Cube MCP Cube MCP Server Cube semantic layer Product and vendor terms Retrieval and memory Infrastructure and deployment analytics mcp semantic layer data A semantic layer for people who would prefer their data agent not to invent the revenue definition during the earnings call.
 Cube is an analytics and semantic-layer platform. Its MCP server lets compatible AI clients connect to Cube so users can ask data questions and receive governed analytics context from the coding or assistant environment.
 It exists because business data is full of definitions, joins, permissions, and metrics that do not belong inside a model's imagination. A semantic layer gives agents a governed way to ask questions against shared business logic.
 It gets treated as if connecting a model to analytics automatically creates trustworthy business intelligence. The quality still depends on the semantic model, access controls, freshness, metric definitions, and whether the question deserved a dashboard rather than a paragraph.
 In practice Cube means exposing governed metrics and analytics workflows to agents through a controlled interface, so data questions can use established definitions instead of freshly hallucinated SQL.
 "The Cube MCP server helped the agent answer the revenue question with the company's definition of revenue, a small miracle in analytics politics."

Cursor Cursor editor AI code editor Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Economics and operations of AI coding developer tools productivity A coding product that lets developers ship in minutes what used to take hours to understand.
 Cursor is an AI-native code editor built around code generation, codebase search, editing, and task-oriented assistance inside a developer workflow.
 Because software work contains a great deal of navigation, repetition, and local pattern matching that models can accelerate, at least until the bill arrives in the form of hidden regressions.
 By treating it as proof that software engineering has become mere prompt entry. Fast edits are real. So are context gaps, bad assumptions, and the need for someone to know what good code looks like.
 In practice it usually means shorter loops on routine code changes, more willingness to touch unfamiliar files, and a fresh need for review discipline because speed arrived before certainty.
 "Cursor handled the refactor in ten minutes. Understanding whether it should have happened still took the afternoon."

Dario Amodei Anthropic CEO Dario Product and vendor terms Safety and evaluation Cultural terms leadership safety governance frontier labs The voice in the room most likely to say the systems may become alarmingly powerful and that we should therefore try not to behave like children with accelerants.
 Dario Amodei is the CEO and co-founder of Anthropic, and one of the most influential public figures in frontier-model development, safety, and AI governance debates.
 Because his name now signals a recognisable stance in AI: build powerful systems, take catastrophic risk seriously, publish the warnings, and still remain very much in the race.
 By casting him either as the uniquely responsible adult in the industry or as merely the most articulate person sprinting toward the same cliff. Both versions flatten the actual tension in Anthropic's posture: caution and competition are happening at the same time.
 When someone invokes Dario Amodei, they usually mean a capability agenda wrapped in unusually explicit language about risk, misuse, and alignment pressure.
 "The paper had a distinctly Dario flavour: measured tone, alarming implications, and the quiet implication that everyone else might be underreacting."
 Is this about Anthropic's commercial posture or its safety posture? Are they pointing to technical risk, governance, or public rhetoric? Is the point that he is more cautious than peers, or simply more explicit? Anthropic Claude Safety Alignment Constitutional AI

Data drift input drift distribution drift Model building Safety and evaluation Economics and operations of AI data monitoring quality inputs The quietly important fact that the world keeps changing even after the model card has been written in a firm and reassuring tone.
 Data drift is the change in the statistical or practical character of input data over time, such that the data seen in production no longer matches the data a model, evaluation set, or workflow was designed around.
 Markets move, users improvise, forms get redesigned, sensors age, policies change, and people discover new ways to be inconsistent. The term exists because models inherit those changes whether the training pipeline feels sentimental about them or not.
 It gets blamed whenever performance falls. Sometimes the inputs truly shifted. Sometimes the labels were weak, the prompt is brittle, the retrieval layer is dirty, or the product team simply changed the job and forgot to tell the model.
 In practice it means monitoring input distributions, segmenting by cohort and source, checking for broken fields and changed formats, and deciding when retraining, prompt changes, or simple data cleaning will solve more than another round of theoretical concern.
 "The fraud model had not become philosophical. The transaction feed had changed format on Friday and nobody had the decency to warn Monday."
 Which features, fields, or user cohorts changed, and when did the change begin? Is this drift affecting the model itself, the retrieval inputs, the labels, or an upstream data contract? Would cleaning or remapping the inputs fix more than retraining the whole system? Data quality Golden dataset Feedback loop

Data Flywheel AI data flywheel feedback flywheel usage flywheel Economics and operations of AI Model building Cultural terms data strategy product training The comforting belief that every user click is secretly training a moat, provided nobody asks about consent, quality, or storage.
 A data flywheel is a feedback loop where product usage creates data that can improve models, features, personalisation, evaluation, or operations, which then attracts more usage and produces more data.
 AI products often improve when they collect better examples of real tasks, failures, preferences, corrections, and outcomes. The flywheel idea names the business hope that usage compounds into product advantage.
 It gets invoked whenever a company has users and a database. Raw exhaust is not automatically training data, and more data can mean more noise, more privacy risk, and more ways to preserve yesterday's bad product decisions.
 In practice it means instrumentation, consent boundaries, data retention rules, labelling workflows, eval sets, privacy review, and a sober answer to whether the collected signal is actually useful for improving anything.
 "The data flywheel looked impressive until the team discovered it was mostly collecting rage clicks from a broken onboarding flow."
 What specific model, eval, or workflow improves because this data exists? Do users know what is being collected and how it will be used? Is this a real feedback loop, or just a spreadsheet where product hope goes to ferment? Feedback loop Product telemetry Data network effect

Data governance  Cultural terms Economics and operations of AI Retrieval and memory data governance cultural economics retrieval The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
 Data governance is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
 It exists because the problems around Shadow AI are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
 It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
 In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

 A label for AI features that perform multi-step searching, reading, synthesis, and citation gathering to produce a more extensive research brief than an ordinary chat response.

DeepSeek DeepSeek-R1 DeepSeek-V3 DeepSeek-V4 DeepSeek-V4-Pro DeepSeek-V4-Flash DeepSeek models Product and vendor terms Model building Economics and operations of AI models competition pricing geopolitics The lab that reminded the market an expensive frontier strategy is still a strategy, not a law of nature.
 DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab and model family associated with high-performing open-weight and reasoning-oriented releases that have influenced pricing, benchmarking, and model strategy across the industry.
 It matters because the field needed a serious reminder that capability can emerge outside the usual American shortlist, and that open releases plus aggressive price-performance claims can reset expectations very quickly.
 It gets used as proof of whatever the speaker already wanted to believe: that frontier spending was absurd, that export controls are irrelevant, or that one viral release settles the economics of the whole sector. It does not.
 In practice it means more pressure on incumbents to justify their pricing, more enterprise curiosity about open-weight deployment, and more geopolitical theatre attached to what is still, at base, a model evaluation problem. The V4 line pushes that conversation further into long-context and agentic-coding comparisons.
 "DeepSeek did not end the frontier race. It merely made several board decks look overpriced overnight."
 Which DeepSeek model and version are we actually discussing? Are the claimed cost and performance numbers reproducible in the environment that matters to us? Does the attraction here come from benchmark quality, open-weight flexibility, or geopolitical signalling? Model pricing war Open-weight competition Chinese model ecosystem The name now refers both to a lab and to a category of market anxiety. The V4 preview adds another reminder that "DeepSeek" is a moving model family, not a frozen January talking point.

Delusion Amplification belief reinforcement loop false-frame escalation Safety and evaluation Cultural terms belief safety reinforcement When the model pours premium fuel onto a bad idea and politely calls it insight.
 A failure mode where a system intensifies a user's false, unstable, or grandiose beliefs by validating assumptions, elaborating narratives, or adding invented support.
 Because language models do not merely answer questions. They can elaborate frames. In the wrong context, elaboration becomes reinforcement.
 By using it for any disagreement with the user or any confident answer. The key issue is compounding false belief, not ordinary error.

Devin Cognition Devin AI software engineer Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Cultural terms coding agents devtools The coding agent that made "replace the engineer" sound briefly easier than "understand the repo."
 Devin is Cognition's AI software engineering product, designed to plan tasks, edit code, run tests, browse documentation, and operate development tooling with limited human supervision.
 Because teams want longer-running coding automation than an inline autocomplete can provide, and investors remain fond of any product that can be described as an engineer in software form.
 By treating benchmark performance or polished demos as proof that the product can safely own a live codebase. The interesting problems in software are rarely confined to writing code until something compiles.
 In practice Devin means delegated development tasks, long-running tool loops, browser and terminal actions, and a new management job involving repo hygiene, task framing, review, and deciding when the agent is confidently wrong.
 "Devin cleared the backlog impressively, if one defines 'cleared' as 'converted into pull requests that still required an adult'."
 Which tasks are actually hands-off, and which still collapse into review-heavy supervision? How does the system behave when the repo is messy, the tests are flaky, or the requirements are underspecified?  DGX is NVIDIA's family of integrated AI systems that bundle high-end GPUs, networking, software, and reference architecture into a packaged platform for training and large-scale inference workloads.
 Because many teams do not want to assemble their own AI compute stack from scratch, and NVIDIA would understandably prefer to sell the whole temple rather than only the candles.

 "Digital worker" is a vendor phrase for automation software, often AI-assisted, that is sold as though it were a bounded employee capable of handling routine business tasks across systems.
 Because buyers understand roles more quickly than architectures, and vendors would rather sell "an accounts payable worker" than "a model-mediated process layer with brittle integrations".
