Distillation knowledge distillation student model training Model building Economics and operations of AI training efficiency compression The art of teaching a smaller model to imitate a larger one, then introducing the result as efficiency rather than inherited opinion.
 Distillation is a training approach in which a smaller or cheaper model learns from the outputs, preferences, or intermediate signals of a larger model so it can reproduce some of its performance at lower cost.
 It exists because frontier-grade behaviour is expensive to serve everywhere. If a smaller system can absorb enough of a stronger model's behaviour, products gain speed, lower inference cost, and a better shot at running locally or at scale.
 It is misused when people describe the resulting model as independent genius rather than compressed imitation. Distillation can preserve capability surprisingly well, but it also preserves blind spots, style, and hidden assumptions.
 In practice it usually means generating a large synthetic training set from a stronger model, fine-tuning a cheaper one on that data, and accepting a careful trade-off between cost, latency, and capability.
 "Their distilled model was impressively cheap, which was another way of saying they had purchased fewer surprises per request."
 What behaviour was the student model trained to copy? Which capabilities held up after distillation, and which quietly fell off? Did the process reduce cost without merely packaging the same errors more efficiently? Teacher model Student model Compression Distillation is often described as model compression, but the interesting part is not just size. It is which behaviours survive the squeeze.

Elon Musk xAI founder Elon Product and vendor terms Cultural terms leadership branding distribution xai What happens when AI acquires a promoter, a distribution engine, and a permanent ability to turn product discussion into spectacle.
 Elon Musk is the founder of xAI and one of the most polarising public figures in AI. In practice, his significance in AI discourse comes from a mix of capital, platform reach, engineering ambition, political attention, and narrative force.
 Because Musk is never merely a person in these discussions. His name functions as a compressed argument about speed, attention, ecosystem control, ideological posture, and whether theatrical force is itself a strategic asset.
 By treating him as either pure substance or pure noise. The useful position is less flattering to everyone's instincts: the spectacle matters because attention, distribution, and organisational velocity all materially affect how AI products land.
 When people invoke Elon Musk in AI, they usually mean xAI's ambitions, the Grok ecosystem, or the broader reality that infrastructure, media reach, and public controversy can be bundled into one strategic instrument.
 "Once Elon entered the product discussion, it acquired three new plotlines, a stronger ideological charge, and a lower chance of remaining about the product."
 Is the relevance technical, political, commercial, or cultural? Are they talking about xAI's systems, Musk's distribution power, or both? Is this claim about model quality, market attention, or theatre with infrastructure attached? xAI Grok Frontier Model Super-apps Date-sensitive point: xAI announced on February 2, 2026 that SpaceX had acquired xAI. For that reason, it is safer to treat Musk here as xAI's founder and dominant public force than to casually repeat older standalone-company titles without context.

Embeddings vector embeddings Retrieval and memory Model usage vectors similarity semantic search The act of turning words, sentences, or images into coordinates so that "similar enough" can be calculated instead of argued about.
 Embeddings are numerical vector representations of data, such as text or images, designed so that semantically similar items are located near one another in vector space. They are commonly used for retrieval, clustering, recommendation, and deduplication.
 Exact keyword matching is often too brittle for messy human language. Embeddings exist so systems can compare meaning approximately rather than matching only literal words.
 They are sometimes spoken about as if they were compact containers of all meaning, truth, and business strategy. They are not. An embedding is useful geometry, not metaphysics. Its quality depends on the model, the data, the task, and the comparison method.
 In practice it usually means encoding text into vectors, storing those vectors somewhere searchable, and using nearest-neighbour techniques to find candidates that look semantically related. They are the quiet workhorses behind a lot of so-called magical retrieval.
 "The search improved after they switched from keywords to embeddings, not because the system got wiser, but because 'refund status' and 'where is my money' finally stopped looking unrelated."
 What embedding model is being used, and for which task? How are similarity, ranking, and filtering being evaluated? What happens when semantically similar items are still operationally irrelevant?

Eval Harness evaluation harness eval runner benchmark harness Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation Model usage evaluation testing agents tooling The machinery teams build once they realise strong opinions about model quality are not, in fact, a release process.
 An eval harness is the framework or runner used to execute repeatable evaluations against models, prompts, tools, or agent workflows and collect comparable results.
 It exists because manual spot checks do not scale, model behaviour drifts, prompts regress quietly, and agent systems fail in more ways than a demo can comfortably admit.
 The harness gets mistaken for truth when it only reflects the cases you bothered to encode. A tidy dashboard is still compatible with spectacular blind spots.
 In practice it usually means versioned datasets, scorers, task traces, cost accounting, regression thresholds, and a growing pile of awkward examples nobody wants to drop because they keep catching something embarrassing.
 "The eval harness did not make the agent good. It merely prevented the team from declaring victory after each plausible-looking run."
 Which real failures are represented in the suite, and which remain folklore? What metrics are being scored automatically, and what still needs human judgement? Does the harness catch regressions before release, or just document them after the fact? Regression suite Offline evaluation Scoring pipeline

Eval-Driven Development EDD eval-first development evaluation-driven development Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows Model usage testing evaluation product regression The radical idea that if your product depends on stochastic behaviour, you should probably measure something before calling the demo a platform.
 Eval-driven development is the practice of using targeted evaluations to guide prompt changes, model selection, tool design, and release decisions for AI systems.
 Traditional software tests do not fully capture AI behaviour, while pure vibes scale poorly once money, customers, or reputations are involved. Teams need a repeatable way to detect regressions and compare alternatives.
 It is misused when a handful of flattering examples are promoted to an evaluation strategy, or when teams treat proxy metrics as if they were the product itself. An eval can discipline judgement. It cannot replace it.
 In practice it usually means curated test sets, grading rubrics, automated checks, and before-after comparisons tied to real tasks. The mature version influences release gates. The immature version produces a dashboard nobody trusts but everyone screenshots.
 "Eval-driven development arrived the day they stopped arguing about prompts in Slack and started arguing about failure rates instead."
 Which behaviours matter enough to measure continuously? Do the evals reflect production tasks, or merely the tasks that are easiest to score? What happens when the metric improves but the user experience quietly worsens? Golden dataset Regression suite Release gate

Evals evaluations evaluation suite Safety and evaluation Model building testing measurement regression The discipline of finding out whether the system works before reality does it for you in public.
 Evals are structured evaluations used to measure model or system performance against defined criteria. They may include benchmark tasks, human review, automated checks, red-team cases, regression tests, or task-specific business metrics.
 Models are stochastic, context-dependent, and often impressive right up until they are deployed. Evals exist to replace anecdote with repeatable evidence and to catch regressions when prompts, models, or orchestration change.
 The word gets thrown around when teams have a benchmark slide and a lot of faith. Real evals are continuous, tied to the actual task, and uncomfortable enough to surface failure. If the evaluation only confirms what the team already hoped, it may be marketing in a lab coat.
 In practice it usually means curated test sets, scored outputs, human raters, regression suites, production monitoring, and painful arguments about what "good enough" means for this use case. Good evals are part of product operations, not just research hygiene.
 "They thought the release was safe because the demo looked smoother. The evals later showed the new prompt quietly doubled the rate of invented citations."
 What failure modes does this eval suite actually test? How often is it run, and what changes trigger regression checks? Are we measuring benchmark elegance or real task performance?

Fine-tuning supervised fine-tuning SFT Model building Model usage adaptation specialisation dataset Taking a large model and sending it to finishing school so it stops answering like the open internet and starts answering like your use case.
 Fine-tuning is the process of further training a pre-existing model on a narrower dataset so that its behaviour shifts toward a specific domain, style, format, or task.
 General-purpose models are broad, not bespoke. Fine-tuning exists for cases where prompting alone is too unstable, too expensive, or too weak to reliably shape behaviour.
 It is often sold as the universal answer whenever prompts disappoint. In reality, many problems blamed on a lack of fine-tuning are retrieval problems, product design problems, or evaluation problems. Fine-tuning can help, but it can also overfit and create fresh failure modes.
 In practice it usually means curating a task-specific dataset, deciding how much of the base model to adapt, training on that smaller dataset, and checking whether the resulting behaviour improved enough to justify the effort and operational complexity.
 "They did not fine-tune because the team loved custom models; they did it because the required output format had to be boringly consistent."
 What behaviour are we trying to change that prompting cannot hold steady? What data will teach that behaviour without teaching unwanted shortcuts? How will we know the tuned model is better, not just narrower?

Frontier Model frontier AI model Model building benchmarks capability status A model said to be at the frontier, which often means "very capable, very expensive, and deeply useful to mention in a fundraising narrative."
 A frontier model is generally understood to mean one of the most capable and resource-intensive models available at a given time, usually near the top end of performance on a range of tasks. It is not a sharply defined scientific category.
 The term exists because the field needed a way to talk about the leading edge of capability without listing every model individually. It can be useful shorthand when discussing high-end systems, their risks, and their costs.
 It is often treated as a formal badge rather than a loose ranking label. Vendors, commentators, and investors may use it to imply inevitability, superiority, or strategic importance beyond what the actual use case warrants.
 In practice it usually means a large, expensive model family with broad capability, strong benchmarks, and significant operational or policy attention. For many products, the question is not whether a model is frontier. The question is whether it is necessary.
 "The company pitched a frontier-model strategy. Operations translated that into a calmer sentence about high cost, external dependency, and unclear product fit."
 Frontier by which metric: benchmark spread, multimodal breadth, tool use, or cost-to-quality ratio? Does the use case actually need a top-tier model, or just a reliable one? What trade-offs come with using the most capable model available?

Function Calling tool calling tool invocation Agents and workflows Model usage tools json actions Giving the model permission to request an action, while reminding everyone that requesting an action is not the same as doing it safely.
 Function calling is a pattern where a model is asked to produce structured arguments for a predefined tool or function, and the surrounding application then executes that tool in ordinary software.
 Models are good at interpreting language but bad at directly mutating real systems without control. Function calling exists to separate decision-making about which action to take from the actual execution of that action.
 It gets described as if the model itself is performing the external action. Usually it is not. The model proposes the call. Your software validates it, executes it, and handles the consequences. If that surrounding code is sloppy, the elegance of the schema will not save you.
 In practice it usually means JSON schemas, argument validation, permission controls, retries, and post-tool reasoning. Useful systems treat function calling as structured intent, not an excuse to trust a model near production infrastructure.
 "The assistant did not refund the customer by itself; it produced a structured request, the service checked policy, and only then did the refund tool run."
 Who validates the arguments before execution? What tools is the model allowed to call, and under what conditions? How are failures, partial successes, and risky actions surfaced?

 The term matters because Google uses Gemini across several surfaces. Without context, claims about Gemini can blur model behaviour, app experience, workspace integration, and vendor strategy.
 It gets treated as if a result seen in one Google surface automatically describes every Gemini model or deployment context. That is rarely true. Tooling, integration, context, and product constraints all change the experience.
 In practice it usually means either a Google model family or a Google product experience using those models. Serious comparison requires pinning down which one.
 "The team said Gemini handled the task well. After some digging, this meant a specific workspace flow, not a portable judgement about every Gemini model."
 Do you mean the Gemini app, a Gemini model family, or a Google product using Gemini behind the scenes? Which version or surface produced the result? What tool access or integration context shaped the outcome?  Gemini conversations often confuse integrated product behaviour with base model ability. Those are related, but not identical, claims.

Gemini 3.1 Flash Live Gemini Flash Live 3.1 Flash Live Gemini audio model Product and vendor terms Model usage Agents and workflows audio voice agents realtime The voice model meant to make real-time conversation feel less like alternating voicemail and more like software that noticed you were still speaking.
 Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is Google's real-time audio and voice model for live dialogue, voice-first agents, and conversational experiences that need lower latency and stronger turn-taking.
 Spoken AI feels broken the moment latency, interruptions, or tonal comprehension fall behind human patience. A live audio model exists because real-time interaction is less forgiving than text and far less polite about failure.
 It gets marketed as natural conversation when many deployments are still glorified phone trees with better diction. Smoother audio helps. It does not automatically produce better reasoning, better policies, or a better answer.
 In practice it means voice agents, customer-support flows, Search Live, and assistant products where handling interruption, noisy environments, and conversational rhythm matters as much as the content of the reply.
 "Flash Live made the assistant sound far more human, which only sharpened the need for the underlying judgement to improve as well."
 Does it handle interruptions and background noise reliably? Which gains come from the model itself, and which come from surrounding orchestration? Are we building a useful voice agent, or simply a smoother queue? Search Live Voice-first agent Turn-taking  Google Gemini Live API Search Live

Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Gemini Flash-Lite 3.1 Flash Lite Product and vendor terms Model usage Economics and operations of AI models latency cost production The cheaper model tier whose chief virtue is letting ambitious product managers scale their optimism by the million tokens.
 Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is Google's lower-cost, lower-latency Gemini 3.1 model for high-volume workloads where throughput and unit economics matter more than maximal frontier capability.
 Most production AI systems are constrained less by grand theory than by latency budgets, API spend, and how much traffic the business intends to survive. A cheaper fast model tier exists because the invoice also attends the architecture review.
 It gets framed as though cheaper automatically means strategically sufficient. Sometimes it does. Sometimes it simply spreads weaker judgement across more requests and gives procurement a pleasant afternoon.
 In practice it means translation, moderation, UI generation, bulk inference, and the usual industrial workloads where modest capability improvements are less important than response speed and cost discipline.
 "Flash-Lite was exactly the right model until someone quietly expanded the use case from 'classify this' to 'think for us'."
 Is the bottleneck latency, token cost, or answer quality? Are we choosing the smallest model that clears the bar, or the cheapest one that lets the slide deck survive? Gemma Google Gemma Gemma models Product and vendor terms Model building Model usage model-family small-models open-weights local-ai Google's way of demonstrating that it, too, can participate in the open-model conversation without misplacing the rest of the product empire.
 Gemma is Google's family of lighter-weight models released for developers to run, fine-tune, or adapt outside the company's main hosted product surfaces.
 Many teams want smaller models for local use, private deployment, research, or fine-tuning. Google also benefits from having a developer-facing model family that can circulate beyond closed API products and keep it visible in the open-model ecosystem.
 It gets confused with Gemini, treated as a single model, or described as if "small" automatically means cheap, safe, or easy to deploy. As usual, the useful details live in the specific variant, size, licence terms, and workload.
 In practice Gemma usually means a family of relatively lightweight Google models for experimentation, local inference, edge deployment, and fine-tuning work where a giant hosted model would be excessive, costly, or simply inconvenient.
 "They chose Gemma for the local prototype because the requirement was responsiveness on-device, not a spiritual commitment to the largest model available."
 Which Gemma variant and size are we evaluating? Does the workload actually benefit from a smaller local model, or are we just allergic to APIs? How do quality, latency, and privacy trade-offs compare with hosted alternatives on the real task? Google Antigravity Antigravity Google AI Studio coding agent Google agentic development platform Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Model usage coding agents developer tools prototyping Google's answer to the question of whether vibe coding needed a launch name with more lift.
 Google Antigravity is Google's agentic development surface for building and editing apps from prompts, with deeper project understanding and integrations for backend, secrets, and deployment-adjacent work.
 Prompt-to-app demos stop being charming once users want authentication, databases, API keys, multiplayer state, and software that can survive contact with actual requirements. Antigravity exists to move that promise closer to full-stack work.
 It is pitched as though software difficulty now consists mainly of choosing a framework and describing the vibes with conviction. The agent can scaffold more of the stack. It cannot repeal complexity, ownership, or future maintenance.
 In practice it means Google AI Studio getting more serious about app generation: Firebase wiring, tool use, framework selection, project memory, and a product surface where the agent is meant to feel like a teammate rather than a chat box with keyboard privileges.
 "Antigravity got the prototype airborne quickly, then left the team to rediscover that landing is its own discipline."
 Is it generating maintainable software or just compressing the prototype stage? How much of the workflow is genuinely agentic rather than pre-scripted scaffolding?  Google DeepMind is Google's central AI research and frontier-model organisation. It combines the original DeepMind organisation with key Google AI research efforts, and sits behind research programmes and products including Gemini, AlphaFold, robotics work, safety research, and broader frontier-model development.
 Because people still say "DeepMind" as if it were a free-floating research monastery. It is now a major unit inside Google, with both scientific prestige and the platform power that comes from sitting inside one of the largest technology companies on earth.
 By treating Google DeepMind as either a pure research institution or merely another product division. It is both more interesting and more awkward than that: a frontier lab nested inside a company that wants research breakthroughs to become products at scale.
 In practice it usually means Google's long-horizon AI engine: the part of the company responsible for major research advances, frontier models, safety work, and many of the technical foundations that later surface through Gemini and other Google products.

Governance posture  Cultural terms Economics and operations of AI governance posture cultural economics relationships The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
 Governance posture is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.

 The term matters because it names a lineage of models built around pre-training and transformer architectures, but it also became a consumer-facing brand fragment attached to products, APIs, and model tiers.

GPT Image 2 OpenAI Images v2 images v2 OpenAI image model v2 Product and vendor terms Model usage images editing openai multimodal OpenAI's current image model for people who would like better picture generation, better editing, and one more meeting about whether prompt writing now counts as art direction.
 GPT Image 2 is OpenAI's current image-generation and image-editing model. It accepts text plus image inputs, returns image outputs, and can be used through the Image API or through the Responses API image generation tool.
 Image systems are now expected to do more than spit out pretty approximations. They need stronger prompt adherence, better editing, higher-fidelity preservation of input images, and a smoother path from one-off generation to iterative product workflows.
 It gets described as though a new image model automatically solves taste, brand judgement, and production readiness. It does not. It mostly means OpenAI has improved the engine while leaving humans in charge of deciding whether the result deserves civilisation.
 In practice it means OpenAI's latest image model for generation and edits, with flexible sizing and stronger image-input handling. The operational wrinkle is that the model itself sits behind two different patterns: direct image endpoints such as `v1/images/generations` and `v1/images/edits`, or a mainline model in the Responses API using the `image_generation` tool for multi-turn editing and prompt revision.
 "They said they had integrated OpenAI's new images v2, which turned out to mean GPT Image 2 plus a mainline model quietly rewriting prompts before the pixels arrived."
 Are you calling the Image API directly, or using the Responses API image generation tool through a mainline model? Do you need high-fidelity editing of an input image, or just one-shot generation? Will the workflow break if someone asks for a transparent background, which GPT Image 2 does not currently support? GPU graphics processing unit AI GPU accelerator card Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI Model usage hardware compute chips infrastructure The rectangle of silicon that turns abstract AI ambition into rack diagrams, queueing pain, and a line item large enough to sober up strategy decks.
 A GPU is a highly parallel processor originally built for graphics work that is now widely used to train and serve AI models because it handles the underlying matrix computations efficiently.
 Because modern AI workloads need a great deal of parallel numerical work, and GPUs became the practical engine for that before most organisations had learned how physical their software plans were.
 By using "GPU" to mean the whole AI stack, or by assuming that access to GPUs automatically solves latency, data quality, product design, or cost discipline. Hardware helps. It does not finish the argument.
 In practice it usually means a constraint on training speed, inference cost, memory limits, model size, procurement timing, and whether your supposedly simple launch now depends on specialist infrastructure.
 "The roadmap called it an AI feature. Finance called it six more GPUs and several uncomfortable follow-up questions."

Grounding grounded generation Retrieval and memory Safety and evaluation evidence retrieval faithfulness The effort to keep a model's answer attached to evidence rather than to whatever polished sentence was statistically easiest to generate.
 Grounding refers to tying a model's output to external evidence, context, or real-world signals so that the answer is based on something more reliable than the model's prior patterns alone.
 Language models can sound certain even when they are improvising. Grounding exists to improve faithfulness by connecting answers to documents, databases, observations, or tool results that bear on the question.
 It gets used as a prestige synonym for "we attached some documents". That is not enough. If the evidence is irrelevant, stale, contradictory, or ignored by the prompt design, the system is not meaningfully grounded.
 In practice it usually means retrieval, citation, tool outputs, or structured data being incorporated in a way that materially constrains the answer. Good grounding is less about adding more text and more about adding the right evidence with clear priority.
 "The grounded version of the assistant did not just answer more cautiously; it cited the actual policy entry it used and admitted when retrieval came back thin."
 What source is the answer grounded in? How is the system encouraged or required to use that evidence faithfully? What happens when the evidence is missing, conflicting, or low quality?  In some research contexts, grounding can refer more broadly to connecting model symbols to the world. In product discussions, it usually means anchoring outputs to evidence or tool results.

gstack Garry Tan's stack AI software factory gstack skills Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Cultural terms agent workflows developer tools qa release The proposition that one founder, one agent shell, and enough specialised slash commands can now impersonate a small product org with alarming speed.
 gstack is Garry Tan's open-source workflow and skill stack for Claude Code and related agent hosts, packaged as a large set of opinionated roles and commands for planning, coding, review, QA, security, release, and retrospectives.
 Because agent users do not just want a model. They want a repeatable operating system for product work: who reviews, who tests, who ships, and what sequence keeps the chaos looking intentional.
 By talking as if the stack itself is a substitute for product judgement, engineering taste, or organisational memory. A thick layer of specialist prompts can accelerate work. It cannot abolish the need to know what good looks like or who is accountable when it is not.
 In practice it means turning an agent shell into a highly structured software-factory workflow, with preset roles for founder review, engineering review, design critique, browser QA, security audit, release management, and post-mortems.
 "The impressive part of gstack was not that it made one person look like a team. The impressive part was how much of the team's ceremony it compressed into commands."
 Which parts are genuinely workflow discipline, and which parts are elaborate prompt theatre? Does the stack improve outcomes on a real repo, or merely increase the amount of agentic motion? Who still owns the decisions once the virtual org chart starts speaking? Claude Code Software factory Review pipeline Garry Tan positions gstack as his personal answer to shipping with agent tooling: a public software-factory kit with role-specific commands, browser QA, review loops, and release mechanics. The interesting question is not whether it feels powerful. The interesting question is whether the structure holds up once the repo gets messy, the product gets real, and the novelty wears off.

Guardrails AI guardrails Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows safety controls permissions The collection of fences, checks, filters, and sternly worded hopes designed to stop a model from wandering into places the product team cannot afford.
 Guardrails are mechanisms used to constrain or monitor model behaviour, such as policy filters, tool permissions, schema validation, prompt rules, moderation systems, human approvals, or output checks.
 Models are flexible in exactly the ways that make product owners nervous. Guardrails exist to narrow behaviour into something safe, compliant, or at least operationally manageable.
 The term gets invoked as though one moderation layer solves the entire problem. Real guardrails are layered, task-specific, and never total. A vague promise of guardrails often means someone has remembered risk late in the process and is trying to sound calmer than they feel.
 In practice it usually means a stack of constraints before, during, and after the model call: restricted tools, validation logic, refusal policies, monitoring, and human review for higher-risk actions. The strongest guardrail is often reducing what the system is allowed to do in the first place.
 "The useful guardrail was not the disclaimer. It was the rule that stopped the assistant from sending anything externally without a human sign-off."
 What specific failure mode is each guardrail intended to catch? Which constraints happen before generation, during generation, and after generation? What evidence shows the current guardrails work on real edge cases?

Hallucination fabrication Safety and evaluation Model usage factuality errors reliability The moment a model says something false with the calm, polished confidence of a consultant who will never have to implement it.
 In AI, a hallucination is an output that is incorrect, fabricated, unsupported, or inconsistent with the source material or task constraints. For language models, this can include made-up facts, invented citations, false summaries, or bogus tool arguments.
 The term exists because generative models produce plausible sequences, not guaranteed truth. They can sound coherent while being wrong, especially when asked beyond their evidence, given poor context, or rewarded for fluent completion rather than faithful reporting.
 It gets used too loosely for every kind of model failure. Not every bad answer is a hallucination. Some outputs are simply outdated, misclassified, poorly retrieved, ambiguously prompted, or badly evaluated. Precision helps because the mitigation often depends on the failure mode.
 In practice it usually means you need better task design, stronger grounding, narrower permissions, tighter output constraints, or human review. It also means you should stop treating eloquence as a proxy for truth.
 "The model produced a five-paragraph answer, three invented legal references, and the exact tone of someone who hoped nobody would check."
 Was the output unsupported by evidence, or was the evidence retrieval itself broken? What level of factual reliability does this workflow actually require? What safeguards exist before the answer reaches a customer, regulator, or executive?

 Hermes Agent is an open-source, MIT-licensed autonomous server agent from Nous Research, positioned around persistent memory, auto-generated skills, scheduled automations, subagents, sandboxing, browser control, and multi-channel access.
 It exists because the agent category is moving from chat windows toward always-on systems that can remember projects, run unattended jobs, delegate work, and operate across communication surfaces.
 It gets tempting to describe the whole package as a digital worker and stop there. The useful conversation is less romantic: what permissions it has, where it runs, which sandboxes apply, what it remembers, and how unattended work is audited.
 In practice it means a self-hosted agent stack for users who want persistent automation, multi-channel interaction, browser and tool access, parallel subagents, and more control than a purely hosted chat product allows.

Inference serving Model usage Infrastructure and deployment serving latency cost The part where the model stops being a research paper and starts being an invoice.
 Inference is the process of running a trained model on new input to produce an output. For language models, that usually means taking tokens in, computing the next-token probabilities, and generating a response token by token.
 The term distinguishes using a model from building one. Training changes weights. Inference uses those weights to do work on fresh data. The distinction matters because the technical constraints, costs, and risks are very different.
 People often use "inference" as a grand-sounding synonym for "the AI bit". That hides the practical questions. Inference is not magic reasoning dust. It is a serving workload with latency, throughput, reliability, and cost characteristics.
 In practice it usually means API calls, GPU time, queueing, prompt assembly, caching, streaming, rate limits, and a finance person eventually asking why the bot became expensive after launch. If your AI feature is live, inference is the meter running.
 "Training happened once in the background; inference happens every time a customer opens the feature and expects an answer in under two seconds."
 What model is doing the inference, where is it running, and who pays per call? Is the bottleneck latency, throughput, quality, or context assembly? Can some of this workload be cached, batched, or offloaded to a smaller model?

Inference Arbitrage model arbitrage inference margin token arbitrage Economics and operations of AI Product and vendor terms Infrastructure and deployment pricing routing margin startups Buying intelligence wholesale from one provider, retailing it through a cleaner interface, and hoping the margin survives long enough to become a strategy.
 Inference arbitrage is the practice of profiting from differences in model price, latency, quality, or access by routing demand across providers and packaging the result as a product or service.
 Model markets are uneven. Providers price differently, regions behave differently, and many users would rather pay one intermediary than personally compare latency tables like a day trader with a prompt addiction.
 The term is stretched when any wrapper, gateway, or comparison site is described as though routing alone were a durable moat. Arbitrage can be real. It is also famously allergic to permanence.
 In practice it usually means mixing models behind the scenes, swapping suppliers when pricing shifts, and capturing some spread through UX, bundling, reliability, or customer ignorance. The better businesses add real workflow value. The thinner ones are mostly tariffs with branding.
 "Their moat turned out to be inference arbitrage with a charming UI and a very aggressive newsletter."
 What part of the margin comes from routing skill, and what part from customer convenience? How quickly does the advantage disappear if providers cut prices or copy the feature? Is there product substance here, or mainly a spread between wholesale tokens and retail confidence? Margin compression Gateway business Wholesale intelligence

Inference Budget token budget serving budget AI runtime budget Economics and operations of AI Infrastructure and deployment Model usage cost serving latency operations The line item where intelligence becomes less abstract and more payable within thirty days.
 An inference budget is the planned or enforced limit on the cost, latency, tokens, model calls, compute, or provider spend used to run AI features in production.
 Model calls have variable cost and performance characteristics. Without a budget, a successful feature can become a financial incident, and an agent can discover new forms of curiosity at the company's expense.
 It gets reduced to token counting. Tokens matter, but so do model choice, retries, tool calls, reasoning effort, cache hits, latency targets, and the number of times a workflow politely tries again.
 In practice it means per-user limits, per-task ceilings, model routing policies, cache strategy, timeout rules, alerting, and deciding which requests deserve the expensive model before the expensive model decides for itself.
 "The inference budget survived launch only after the agent was stopped from spending premium reasoning on password reset emails."
 What is the maximum acceptable cost for this user action? Which requests can use a cheaper model, cache, or shorter reasoning path? Are retries and tool calls counted in the same budget as the visible model response? Serving economics Cost controls Runtime policy

Inference Provider model host inference platform model serving provider Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI Product and vendor terms infrastructure api hosting pricing The company renting you access to intelligence by the token, with latency graphs where the soul used to be.
 An inference provider runs and serves AI models through APIs or managed infrastructure so customers can send inputs and receive outputs without operating the full model stack themselves.
 Because reliable model serving requires GPUs, batching, scaling, observability, routing, and a tolerance for operational pain that many product teams would rather outsource.
 By blurring the line between building a model and hosting one, or by describing a rented endpoint as proprietary intelligence simply because the invoice arrives under a different logo.
 In practice the choice of inference provider shapes latency, price, regional availability, data handling, model access, and how much of your application's reliability now depends on somebody else's serving layer.
 "They said they had their own AI platform, which turned out to mean an inference provider, a neat dashboard, and a heroic willingness to rename dependency as strategy."
 Who actually trains the model, and who merely serves it? What happens to cost and performance when traffic scales? Which data-handling guarantees survive the extra layer? Model hosting Managed serving Vendor dependency

Inference Stack LLM ops stack inference platform serving stack Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI Model usage infrastructure serving cost The collection of expensive plumbing discovered immediately after somebody says, "It's just one API call."
 An inference stack is the set of systems used to run model requests in production, including providers, routing, caching, prompt assembly, guardrails, observability, evaluation, and cost control.
 Because shipping AI features requires more than model access. Real applications need reliability, latency control, policy enforcement, failover, measurement, and a defensible explanation for the monthly bill.
 By calling every thin wrapper a stack. A real inference stack handles production constraints. A dropdown of model names and a vibes-based retry loop do not amount to infrastructure.
 In practice it means SDKs, proxies, model routers, rate limits, prompt templates, redaction, logging, eval pipelines, cache policies, billing dashboards, and repeated arguments over whether quality improved enough to justify the spend.
 "The inference stack matured rapidly once finance started reading the token invoices with feeling."
 Which parts of the stack protect reliability, and which merely create another place for failure to hide? How are routing, caching, and fallback policies affecting quality as well as cost? Who can explain the per-request economics without opening six dashboards and a prayer? Serving layer Production LLM ops Request routing

Jailbreak prompt jailbreak model jailbreak guardrail bypass Safety and evaluation Model usage Cultural terms safety security prompts policy The art of persuading a safety system that the rules were meant for someone else.
 A jailbreak is a prompt or interaction pattern intended to bypass a model's safety rules, system instructions, or refusal policies and elicit behaviour it is supposed to restrict.
 It exists because instruction-following systems are probabilistic, layered defences are imperfect, and many users are curious, mischievous, adversarial, or simply unwilling to accept "no" from a machine.
 The term gets stretched to cover any successful prompt tweak. More precisely, a jailbreak is about overriding the intended boundary, not merely asking the question more clearly.
 In practice it means red-team exercises, defence updates, moderation tuning, prompt hardening, and a permanent reminder that a polite interface does not imply a secure one, especially once tools and external systems are attached.
 "The jailbreak was funny right up to the moment someone remembered the model also had access to tools."
 What boundary was bypassed: policy, system prompt, tool permission, or all three? Was this a curiosity, a reproducible exploit, or a path to real-world harm? How quickly can the defence be patched without breaking ordinary use? Policy bypass Adversarial prompting Safety boundary Not every refusal workaround is a serious security event. The risk changes sharply when the model can call tools, access data, or operate in regulated workflows.

Knowledge Cutoff cutoff date knowledge date model cutoff Core concepts Model usage Cultural terms recency freshness model knowledge The calendar line after which the model becomes a gifted historical novelist.
 A knowledge cutoff is the latest point in time reflected in a model's internal knowledge without fresh retrieval or live access to newer information.
 Models are built from data gathered during a finite period. However broad that period was, it still ends somewhere, which means the model's default knowledge eventually becomes stale.
 By treating the cutoff as either a precise magical boundary or as something that no longer matters because the interface looks current. A model can sound up to date long after its internal map stopped being refreshed.
 In practice it usually means any question about recent events, changing laws, product releases, prices, or people who change jobs needs fresh sources instead of trusting the model's memory and posture.
 "The answer sounded current until someone checked the date. It was confidence wearing a knowledge cutoff."
 What information in this workflow changes often enough to outrun the model's built-in knowledge? Is the system using fresh retrieval, or merely sounding well informed about the past? How would we notice if the answer stopped at last year's world? Current information Recency Grounding

Liar's Dividend liar dividend synthetic deniability deepfake alibi Cultural terms Safety and evaluation deepfakes trust media verification The social profit earned when fake media becomes plausible enough that real evidence can be waved away as synthetic inconvenience.
 The liar's dividend is the benefit people gain when the existence of convincing AI-generated or manipulated media lets them deny authentic evidence as fake.
 AI has lowered the cost of producing persuasive synthetic audio, images, and video. That does not only create fake evidence. It also weakens confidence in real evidence by giving bad actors a ready-made excuse.
 The phrase can be used too theatrically, as though trust has already collapsed beyond recovery. Verification, provenance, and forensic methods still matter. The problem is not total epistemic ruin. It is a higher and more expensive burden of proof.
 In practice it usually means journalists, courts, platforms, and the public need stronger provenance checks while opportunists claim every inconvenient recording is probably AI. The immediate damage is often delay, confusion, and public doubt rather than perfect deception.
 "The clip was real, but the liar's dividend arrived on schedule and turned basic verification into a partisan hobby."
 What provenance or verification evidence accompanies this media? Who benefits from uncertainty around its authenticity? Are we discussing actual forensic doubts, or opportunistic denial dressed as prudence? Content provenance Forensic verification Authenticity crisis

 LLM-as-a-judge is an evaluation pattern in which one language model scores, ranks, or compares outputs from another model against a rubric, preference signal, or pairwise choice.
 Because human evaluation is expensive, slow, and inconveniently unwilling to scale at the pace product teams prefer.
 By treating the judge as neutral, objective, or magically aligned with user value. A model grader can inherit stylistic bias, provider bias, verbosity bias, and a remarkable affection for whatever sounds polished.

MCP Model Context Protocol Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment protocol tools resources A way of making tools and resources speak a common dialect so the assistant stops needing a bespoke adapter for every cupboard in the house.
 MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a protocol for exposing tools, resources, and related capabilities to AI clients in a standardised way, so assistants can discover and use external systems without each integration being entirely custom.
 AI applications increasingly need access to files, APIs, documentation, databases, prompts, and tools. Without a shared protocol, every client-server pairing becomes its own integration project. MCP exists to reduce that fragmentation.
 It gets spoken about as though a protocol itself makes systems intelligent, secure, or interoperable by magic. It does not. Standardisation helps, but permissions, trust boundaries, authentication, and product design still matter more than the acronym.
 In practice it usually means exposing capabilities through a consistent interface so a client can list resources, call tools, and work with external context more systematically. The real value is reducing bespoke glue and making integrations more legible.
 "MCP did not solve the assistant's judgement problem. It solved the quieter and more useful problem of not writing one-off connectors forever."

Memory Policy memory rules memory retention policy Retrieval and memory Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation memory retention privacy The quiet constitution deciding what the system remembers, for how long, and which future mistake inherits the privilege.
 Memory policy is the set of rules governing what an AI system stores from interactions, how long it keeps that information, when it retrieves it, and when it must forget or redact it.
 It exists because continuity is useful and indiscriminate memory is dangerous. Long-running systems can become more helpful by remembering preferences, facts, and prior decisions, but they can also become more wrong, more invasive, and more difficult to debug.
 The term gets reduced to "we have memory now", as if any retained context were inherently intelligent. It also gets used to dress up ordinary logging or vector storage without answering the harder questions about consent, correction, deletion, and sensitive data.
 In practice it means retention windows, summarisation rules, provenance, retrieval thresholds, redaction filters, user controls, and clear logic for when stale memory should be ignored rather than honoured out of misplaced loyalty.
 "The assistant felt personal right up until its memory policy allowed one outdated preference to haunt every recommendation for the next three months."
 What information qualifies for memory, and what must never be stored? How are stale or incorrect memories corrected, deleted, or superseded? When memory is retrieved, how does the system distinguish relevance from sentimental hoarding? Personalisation Retention policy Profile memory

Mixture of Experts MoE expert routing Core concepts Model building Infrastructure and deployment architecture routing efficiency An architecture for having enormous parameter counts without insisting every token use the whole cathedral.
 A mixture-of-experts model is a neural architecture in which a routing mechanism activates only a subset of specialised sub-networks for each token or input, allowing higher total capacity without using all parameters on every pass.
 Because model builders want more capacity and performance without paying the full compute cost of dense models at every step. Sparse activation offers a way to scale capability more economically, at least in theory.
 By treating "experts" as if the model contains little professors with stable domains of knowledge. It is an optimisation strategy, not a panel discussion. The routing can help a great deal, but it does not grant wisdom by committee.
 In practice it usually means better parameter-efficiency on paper, more complex training and serving behaviour, routing overhead, and a fresh set of engineering questions about load balancing, latency spikes, and uneven quality.
 "The model was a mixture of experts, which sounded philosophical until the serving team had to explain the routing bottlenecks."
 How many experts exist, and how many are active per token? What did sparse routing improve relative to a dense baseline at the same cost? Did the architecture move the bottleneck from compute to systems complexity? Sparse model Routing layer Dense model

Model Collapse synthetic data collapse recursive training collapse Model building Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms data quality feedback The fear that if the machines keep learning from one another's homework, the class average will eventually become the curriculum.
 Model collapse is the hypothesised degradation that can occur when models are trained repeatedly on synthetic or model-generated data without enough fresh, high-quality human data to preserve diversity and fidelity.
 Because synthetic data is useful, cheap, and increasingly tempting at scale, but feedback loops can distort distributions, wash out edge cases, and quietly teach systems to imitate their own errors.
 By treating it as a guaranteed apocalypse or as proof that synthetic data is worthless. Synthetic data can be valuable. The concern is about unmanaged feedback loops, weak provenance, and replacing judgement with volume.
 In practice it usually means teams need stronger data lineage, better curation, and honest evaluation before they let generated material dominate the training mix just because it is abundant and conveniently machine-readable.
 "The lab was not worried about synthetic data in principle. It was worried about model collapse from feeding the next system a buffet of the previous system's bad habits."
 How much of the training mix is synthetic, and how is its quality measured? What prevents generated noise from becoming self-confirming doctrine? Are we preserving human-created edge cases or optimising them out of existence? Data provenance Feedback loop Distribution shift

Model drift behaviour drift model behaviour drift Safety and evaluation Model usage Economics and operations of AI evaluation monitoring operations behaviour The moment a system that used to behave one way quietly acquires new manners, new vices, or a fresh interpretation of your instructions, and no dashboard feels personally responsible.
 Model drift is the change in a model system's behaviour over time, whether because the underlying model changed, surrounding prompts and tools changed, traffic changed, or the environment stopped being the one the original evaluation had in mind.
 AI systems are not static artefacts. Providers update models, teams revise prompts, tools change their schemas, and user behaviour wanders off. The term exists because quality can move even when nobody thinks they touched the main lever.
 It gets used as a polite synonym for "something got worse". Sometimes the issue is genuine behaviour drift. Sometimes it is bad retrieval, a broken tool, a prompt edit, or a team discovering that last month's benchmark did not survive contact with this month's traffic.
 In practice it means running comparisons over time, holding out stable evaluation sets, watching sensitive slices rather than averages, and keeping enough version history to identify whether the model changed or the system around it did.
 "The team called it model drift because that sounded dignified. The incident report later revealed a prompt change, a tool timeout, and optimism."
 Which part of the system changed: the base model, the prompt stack, the tools, the routing, or the traffic? What stable evaluation set shows the behaviour actually moved rather than merely encountering a harder week? Can we roll back or replay the previous configuration to isolate the change? Regression suite Quality gate Operational telemetry

 Because buyers want more than one model, clouds want to be the place where that choice gets made, and nobody enjoys integrating a fresh API every time the leaderboard twitches.
 By making model access sound like curation solved the hard part. A catalogue is useful. It does not answer which model is trustworthy, economical, regionally available, or appropriate for a real workload.
 In practice it usually means a procurement-and-deployment layer with billing, quotas, IAM, regions, logging, and just enough abstraction to turn model shopping into infrastructure policy.
 "The model garden looked abundant until the architecture review reduced it to the three options legal, security, and latency could tolerate."

Model Router routing layer multi-model router AI gateway Model usage Agents and workflows Economics and operations of AI routing multi-model cost The switchboard that promises the right model for every job and occasionally adds a thrilling new place for policy mistakes to hide.
 A model router is a software layer that selects among multiple models or providers for a given request based on rules or signals such as task type, latency, cost, availability, policy, or fallback behaviour.
 Because no single model is best at everything, outages happen, prices move, and product teams would prefer not to hard-code every bet into the application layer.
 By marketing routing itself as intelligence, or by implying that a multi-model stack is automatically well evaluated. A router can express good operational judgement. It can also just spread uncertainty across more vendors.
 In practice it usually means heuristics, policy rules, retries, provider abstractions, observability, and a permanent negotiation between quality, speed, and the monthly bill.
 "The model router kept the app online during the outage, then introduced a fresh round of debugging because two providers interpreted the same tool schema differently."
 What signals drive routing decisions, and were they benchmarked on real workloads? What is the fallback policy when the preferred model fails or drifts? Does the router reduce operational risk, or merely distribute it? Provider abstraction Fallback policy Traffic shaping Vendors often sell model routers as a neutral convenience layer. In practice the interesting part is what routing policy the customer can inspect, override, and trust.

Model spec behaviour spec model behaviour specification Product and vendor terms Safety and evaluation Model usage policy behaviour providers governance A public document describing how the model ought to behave, which is at least more honest than pretending the behaviour emerged from pure mathematics and good breeding.
 A model spec is a provider-authored behavioural specification that describes the intended priorities, refusal patterns, boundaries, and instruction-handling rules for a model or model family.
 Modern models are expected to be useful, safe, steerable, and publicly defensible at once. A specification exists so providers can state what behaviour they are trying to produce and give developers a reference for the rules sitting behind the pleasant interface.
 It gets treated as a guarantee, a safety proof, or a substitute for evaluation. A spec can clarify intent. It cannot ensure that every model version, product surface, or edge case will obey the text as faithfully as legal and marketing might prefer.
 In practice it means reading the provider's stated priorities, understanding how instruction hierarchy and refusals are supposed to work, and designing prompts, product expectations, and escalation paths around the fact that the model has house rules you did not personally write.
 "The product team called it a bug. The model spec called it a refusal. The user called it a waste of time."
 Which behaviours in this product come from our prompt stack, and which come from the provider's published rules? How closely does the live model match the current spec in the cases we actually care about? What happens operationally when the provider updates the spec before we update our expectations? System card Responsible release Capability boundaries

Multimodal multimodal model Core concepts Model usage image audio video Able to juggle text, images, audio, or video, which sounds far more coherent than the reality of several data types sharing one product team.
 Multimodal refers to models or systems that can process, generate, or jointly reason over more than one data modality, such as text, image, audio, or video.
 Human tasks rarely arrive as clean text alone. Multimodal systems exist because many useful applications involve documents, screenshots, spoken language, visual context, or combinations of these.
 It gets waved around as a prestige adjective even when a product merely accepts an image upload and produces a caption. True multimodal capability is not just accepting multiple inputs. It is being able to use them meaningfully for the task at hand.
 In practice it usually means a model or stack that can ingest several types of data, map them into compatible internal representations, and produce outputs that reflect those combined signals. The hard part is often not modality support itself but maintaining reliability across them.
 "The feature was multimodal in the useful sense: it could read the chart, listen to the spoken explanation, and answer questions about the mismatch."
 Which modalities can the system actually handle in production? Does the model merely accept multiple formats, or does it use them jointly to improve the outcome? How has performance been evaluated across the different input types?  Multimodal can describe both input and output capabilities. A system that reads images and writes text is multimodal, even if it does not generate images in return.

Neocloud GPU cloud startup AI-native cloud GPU landlord Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI cloud gpu infrastructure startups A cloud provider whose most disruptive feature is often discovering that scarce Nvidia hardware can be resold with excellent posture.
 "Neocloud" is an informal label for newer cloud providers focused on AI infrastructure, especially GPU capacity for training and inference, rather than broad general-purpose enterprise cloud offerings.
 The term exists because demand for accelerators outran easy supply, while many teams wanted alternatives to the hyperscalers on price, access, support, geography, or simple willingness to answer the phone.
 It is misused whenever specialised capacity is treated as a permanent moat. Owning or brokering GPUs can be valuable. It does not automatically solve networking, reliability, data governance, procurement, or the small inconvenience of debt.
 In practice it usually means a provider that can get you machines faster, sometimes cheaper, sometimes with better human support, in exchange for narrower platform breadth and a closer relationship with the physics of hardware finance.
 "The neocloud pitch was compelling right up to the moment finance noticed the business model involved both GPUs and optimism."
 Are we choosing this provider for price, access, support, location, or all four? What happens to reliability, networking, and compliance once we leave the big clouds? Is this a real platform advantage or an opportunistic position in a supply shortage? GPU cloud Hyperscaler alternative Compute scarcity The term describes a market position more than a technical architecture. Some neoclouds are serious platforms. Some are very earnest forwarding businesses with GPUs.

NotebookLM Notebook LM Google NotebookLM source-grounded notebook Product and vendor terms Retrieval and memory Model usage research notes documents retrieval The unusually sensible AI product that became popular by admitting you should probably start with your own documents.
 NotebookLM is Google's source-grounded research and note-taking product for uploading or linking material, asking questions about it, generating summaries, and producing derivative outputs such as briefs or audio overviews.
 Most knowledge work does not need an omniscient machine performing personality. It needs help digesting a bounded set of files, links, transcripts, slides, or notes without wandering off into generic internet mush.
 It gets mistaken for a citation guarantee or a truth engine. Grounding in supplied sources materially helps, but the system can still overstate, misconnect, or present generated synthesis with more poise than certainty.
 In practice NotebookLM means a polished retrieval-and-synthesis interface for source packs: useful for study guides, research briefings, interview prep, internal manuals, and turning document piles into something a human can survive.
 "NotebookLM did not make the report magically correct, but it did make the document swamp considerably less vindictive."
 Which sources are actually in the notebook, and which claims come from outside them? Is the value in source-grounded synthesis, or are we quietly expecting general web research as well? Would a document search tool be enough, or do we genuinely need generated structure on top? Source pack Audio Overview Research assistant NotebookLM is strongest when the source set is bounded and relevant, not when it is treated as a landfill with a search box.

NPU neural processing unit AI accelerator on-device AI chip Infrastructure and deployment Model usage Product and vendor terms hardware on-device silicon privacy The dedicated silicon every device launch now invokes to prove the future has arrived locally, efficiently, and preferably in this quarter's hardware cycle.
 An NPU is a specialised processor designed to accelerate machine-learning workloads, especially on-device inference tasks such as speech, vision, transcription, or smaller language-model features.
 Because CPUs and GPUs are not always the best fit for constant local inference, and device makers want better performance per watt for AI features that cannot afford cloud latency or cloud disclosure.
 By treating the presence of an NPU as evidence that meaningful AI features exist, or by presenting the acronym as a product benefit without saying what models fit, what latency improves, or what the software stack can actually use.
 In practice an NPU determines which features can run on-device, how responsive and private they can be, and whether "AI PC" or "AI phone" means a new capability or merely a fresh sticker on the box.
 "The keynote celebrated the NPU at length, which was useful because the list of software features still fit comfortably on one slide."
 Which models or tasks actually run on the NPU today? What latency, battery, or privacy gains are measurable rather than implied? Is the developer stack mature enough to use the hardware well? On-device inference Edge compute Silicon marketing

NVIDIA Nvidia NVIDIA platform Product and vendor terms Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI chips compute gpu infrastructure A chip company that became the closest thing the AI boom has to a central bank, except louder and with better leather jackets.
 NVIDIA is a computing platform company best known in AI for GPUs, accelerated computing systems, and the broader hardware-software stack used to train and serve many modern AI models.
 Because AI keeps pretending to be abstract until someone has to pay for the compute. NVIDIA matters because the current boom runs on its chips, systems, software ecosystem, and the procurement gravity that follows them.
 By using NVIDIA as shorthand for AI itself. That exaggerates the company and erases the rest of the stack, but it does capture where a great deal of pricing power and strategic leverage currently sits.
 In practice it usually means GPUs, DGX-class systems, CUDA-shaped ecosystems, inference clusters, and the hardware economics under both frontier training and production deployment.
 "The board wanted an AI strategy. The operations team, less poetically, wanted to know how much of it would end up being tribute to NVIDIA."
 Is this a chip discussion, a systems discussion, or a software-ecosystem discussion? Are we bottlenecked by compute, cost, availability, or habit? Could another stack do the job, or are we already too CUDA-shaped to be honest about the answer? Jensen Huang GPU Training Inference DGX NVIDIA's own framing increasingly presents the company as the engine of AI and the builder of AI factories. The important practical point is that it is no longer just a component supplier; it is a stack with enormous ecosystem gravity.

NVIDIA NIM NIM NVIDIA NIM APIs build.nvidia.com NIM microservices Infrastructure and deployment Product and vendor terms Model usage inference serving nvidia deployment NVIDIA's reminder that once everyone wants models in production, the pick-and-shovel business starts selling neatly boxed shovels.
 NVIDIA NIM is a set of microservices and API surfaces for deploying, serving, and trying foundation models across NVIDIA's AI stack, including hosted NIM APIs exposed through build.nvidia.com.
 It exists because serving models is not just a model choice. Teams also need runtimes, endpoints, containers, security updates, deployment patterns, and enough operational packaging to make inference feel less handmade.
 It gets treated as if the presence of an official endpoint solves model selection, quality evaluation, latency budgets, or vendor lock-in. A production-grade serving wrapper still serves whatever decision you made inside it.
 In practice it usually means easier access to NVIDIA-optimised model serving, hosted trials, and deployment recipes for teams already thinking in GPUs, data centres, clouds, and enterprise support contracts.

Ollama local model runner Ollama server local inference runtime Product and vendor terms Infrastructure and deployment Model usage local-ai self-hosting developer-tools inference The software that persuaded a great many people to download a model locally and call the resulting fan noise a privacy strategy.
 Ollama is a tool and runtime for downloading, serving, and interacting with local AI models through a straightforward CLI and API on a developer machine or private system.
 Running local models used to feel more like a weekend project than a developer workflow. Ollama exists because many teams wanted a simple way to try local inference, private prototypes, and offline model use without assembling the whole stack by hand.
 It gets mistaken for a complete production strategy or proof that local inference is automatically secure, cheap, or enterprise-ready. Pulling a model locally is helpful. It does not abolish memory limits, observability, scaling, or the possibility that the chosen model is merely mediocre in private.
 In practice it usually means convenient local model packaging for experiments, prototypes, internal tools, and developer workflows. It is especially useful for reducing friction around self-hosted testing, less useful as a substitute for a real serving platform once traffic, governance, or uptime begin to matter.
 "They rolled the demo on Ollama because the requirement was a private proof of concept, not a treatise on distributed inference."
 Which model and quantisation are we actually running? Is local inference solving privacy, latency, cost, or merely setup friction? Do we need a developer runtime, or do we really need a production serving stack? Local AI Self-hosting Model packaging The term matters because it lowered the activation energy for local AI so dramatically that many more people could form opinions before involving procurement.

One-Person Unicorn solo unicorn AI solopreneur unicorn single-founder unicorn Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms Agents and workflows founders labour startups automation The recurring fantasy that one founder, several agents, and a cloud invoice can replace an organisation and still count as a company instead of an episode.
 The "one-person unicorn" is the idea that AI tools and automation could let a single founder build and run a company worth a billion dollars with little or no traditional staff.
 It exists because AI has genuinely reduced the cost of coding, design, support, research, and back-office work enough for solo founders to reach farther than they could a few years ago. That makes the old dream of extreme leverage newly discussable and newly distortive.
 The term gets used as if headcount were simply a regrettable inefficiency. It understates the boring things companies are still made of: trust, sales, operations, compliance, customer support, and the general inconvenience of reality needing more than one pair of eyes.
 In practice it usually means smaller teams doing more, solo founders getting to revenue faster, and investors testing how much organisational fragility they can rename as leverage.
 "The one-person unicorn thesis sounds marvellous until the founder discovers that support, tax, contracts, and incident response still decline to become vibes."
 Which parts of the company are actually automated, and which parts are being silently done by contractors, software, or wishful thinking? Does the business model scale without a team, or only the demo? What breaks first when a supposedly solo company meets regulation, churn, or enterprise procurement? Solo founder Operating leverage Invisible enterprise The important point is not whether the perfect solo billion-dollar company appears. The important point is that a lot of people now behave as if it is imminent.

Open Weight open weights weight release Product and vendor terms Model building Economics and operations of AI licensing models distribution The compromise term for when a company wants the glow of openness without the administrative burden of actually being open source.
 An open-weight model is one whose trained parameters are released for others to download and run, even if the training code, data, licences, or usage rights remain restricted.
 The term exists because there is a meaningful difference between sharing a model's weights and sharing the full ingredients behind it. The industry needed language for that middle ground, if only to avoid calling everything open source with a straight face.
 It is misused whenever "open weight" is presented as equivalent to full transparency, unrestricted reuse, or community governance. A downloadable checkpoint can still arrive with significant legal, technical, and commercial strings attached.
 In practice it usually means you can run or fine-tune the model yourself, but you may not know precisely how it was trained, what data shaped it, or what terms govern your most lucrative use case.
 "The vendor called it open weight, which was accurate enough if you ignored the licence, the data secrecy, and the very expensive preferred hosting arrangement."
 What exactly has been released besides the checkpoint? What restrictions apply to commercial use, redistribution, or modification? Can the model be audited meaningfully, or only downloaded? Open source Source available Model licence

OpenAI OpenAI company Product and vendor terms Core concepts vendor company api A company name frequently used as shorthand for an app, a model family, an API, a policy debate, and sometimes the entire industry when people cannot be bothered to separate nouns.
 OpenAI is a company. It builds and sells AI products and services, including model APIs and end-user products such as ChatGPT. The company name is distinct from the names of its individual models and products.
 The term matters because company, product, and model are constantly collapsed into one another. If you do not separate them, conversations about performance, pricing, safety, procurement, and market position become foggy very quickly.
 People say "OpenAI said this" when they mean ChatGPT produced a reply, or "we use OpenAI" when they actually use a specific API model through a specific product flow. The confusion sounds minor until accuracy, vendor risk, or budgeting starts mattering.
 In practice it usually means a commercial vendor relationship. You are choosing a supplier, a set of models, platform terms, product constraints, and an evolving roadmap. The most important question is usually not whether OpenAI exists, but which exact OpenAI product or model you mean.
 "The board memo said the company was 'using OpenAI', which turned out to mean one customer-service workflow calling a single model through the API with very strict prompt scaffolding."
 Do you mean the company, the API platform, ChatGPT, or a specific model? What dependency does the product have on this vendor: model quality, tooling, pricing, or distribution? What is the fallback plan if the model, terms, or costs change?   ChatGPT OpenAI API GPT model families

Orchestration workflow orchestration Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment workflow automation state The noble art of arranging prompts, tools, retries, rules, and side effects so that a demo looks like intelligence rather than plumbing.
 Orchestration is the software layer that coordinates model calls, tool use, retrieval, validation, branching logic, retries, and state across a workflow. It determines how the surrounding system manages the model, not what the model intrinsically knows.
 Modern AI products are rarely a single model call. They are systems. Orchestration exists because useful behaviour often depends on ordering steps, handling failures, and deciding when to ask a model versus when to run conventional software.
 The term gets abused when simple glue code is dressed up as autonomous reasoning. Sometimes orchestration is just sensible application logic wearing a tuxedo because "if statements and APIs" sounded too ordinary for the pitch deck.
 In practice it usually means a chain of decisions around prompts, routing, tool permissions, schema checks, retrieval steps, model selection, human approvals, and error handling. Good orchestration makes systems reliable. Bad orchestration makes them busy.
 "The impressive part was not the model; it was the orchestration layer that knew when to search the docs, when to call billing, and when to stop pretending confidence."
 What steps are being orchestrated, and which of them actually require a model? Where does state live between steps? What happens when a tool call fails, returns nonsense, or arrives too late?

Personal Intelligence Google Personal Intelligence connected context personal context mode Product and vendor terms Retrieval and memory Model usage personalisation memory search privacy The promise that your assistant becomes more useful once it is permitted a supervised rummage through your digital life.
 Personal Intelligence is Google's feature for connecting apps like Gmail and Photos to Gemini or AI Mode so the system can answer with more personal context.
 Generic assistants plateau quickly. Connected assistants can answer questions that only make sense against your own history, purchases, plans, and messages. The term exists because context is now a product feature, not merely a prompt length problem.
 It is framed as pure helpfulness when the real proposition is intimacy with guardrails. Opt-in controls matter, but the underlying bargain is still clear: give the system more of your life and hope the convenience is worth the access.
 In practice it means personalised itineraries, recommendations, memory-like assistance across Google products, and a new set of design and trust questions about permissions, boundaries, and how much context people really want their assistant to hold.
 "Personal Intelligence made the travel plan far more relevant, which was lovely right up until everyone remembered what relevance had to be fed."
