Physical AI embodied AI robotics AI world-facing AI Product and vendor terms Cultural terms Model usage robotics automation deployment hardware The rebranding of robotics for an era in which software valuations discovered they might need a body after all.
 "Physical AI" is a broad industry label for applying modern AI systems to embodied machines such as robots, vehicles, and industrial equipment operating in real environments.
 The term exists because language and image models do not move boxes, navigate warehouses, or survive factory floors by themselves. Embodied systems need perception, control, planning, simulation, and safety engineering tied to physical constraints.
 It is misused whenever a polished robot demo is treated as evidence that deployment is near. Hardware, safety, reliability, maintenance, and integration remain stubbornly unimpressed by keynote vocabulary.
 In practice it usually means expensive sensor stacks, simulation pipelines, teleoperation fallbacks, long deployment cycles, and a great deal of engineering dedicated to the fact that the physical world does not accept almost right.
 "The physical AI roadmap sounded transformative until operations asked who would service the fleet after the first enthusiastic collision."
 What part of the system is actually learned, and what part is conventional robotics? How does the system fail in messy real environments rather than curated demos? What are the safety, maintenance, and integration costs after the pilot ends? Embodied AI Robotics stack Autonomy deployment

 Pieter Levels is an indie maker known for products such as Nomad List and Remote OK and, in contemporary AI culture, for representing the argument that one person with distribution, taste, and AI tooling can build more than many small teams would care to admit.
 Because some names stop referring only to a person and start referring to a method. Invoking Levels usually means shipping in public, bootstrapping, keeping teams tiny, and treating software as something to release before the deck is finished apologising.
 By turning an unusual operator with an audience into a universal organisational theory. His style is instructive, but it does not erase survivorship bias, domain complexity, support work, or the awkward truth that most founders are not Pieter Levels with a timeline full of customers.
 In practice, invoking Levels usually means solo-founder leverage, fast iteration, blunt market testing, and a belief that AI tools amplify people who already know how to ship, write, and sell.
 "The moment someone invoked @levelsio, the roadmap became less a plan than an accusation that the company might simply have too many meetings."
 Are they borrowing his execution style, or just his mythology? Is the lesson about distribution, speed, team size, or public accountability? Would this approach still hold once the product had enterprise customers, compliance, or a support queue? Indie hacker Build in public Solo founder His own site uses the name Pieter Levels, even though people online often flatten it to Peter Levels. The distinction is minor; the mythology is not.

Pilot Purgatory pilot hell proof-of-concept limbo permanent pilot Economics and operations of AI Agents and workflows Cultural terms pilots adoption operations The budgetary afterlife in which every firm proves its boldness by never quite deploying anything.
 A recurring organisational state in which AI initiatives remain stuck in trials, proofs of concept, and limited rollouts without becoming trusted, scaled operations.
 Because demos are easier than deployment, sponsors like novelty more than process change, and real adoption usually requires data access, security review, staff enablement, governance, and someone to own the outcome.
 By calling endless experimentation strategy. A pilot is a learning phase. It becomes purgatory when the organisation wants the optics of innovation without paying the price of commitment.
 In practice it usually means many vendor meetings, a few polished demos, no durable workflow change, and a graveyard of licences nobody remembers approving.
 "The company had twelve AI pilots and one AI workflow in production. Pilot purgatory was not a risk; it was the operating model."
 What concrete condition must be met for this pilot to graduate or die? Who owns the workflow after the novelty budget runs out? What operational change is the organisation avoiding by calling this an experiment? Change management Procurement Operational rollout

Post-Training posttraining alignment tuning instruction tuning pipeline Model building Safety and evaluation Core concepts tuning alignment models All the work done after pretraining so the base model can stop sounding raw, evasive, or commercially inconvenient.
 Post-training is the phase after base-model pretraining where a model is further adapted through methods such as supervised fine-tuning, preference optimisation, safety tuning, distillation, tool-use training, or reasoning-focused tuning.
 Because a pretrained model may have broad knowledge yet still be poor at following instructions, using tools, refusing unsafe requests, formatting outputs, or behaving in ways customers and policy teams can live with.
 By treating it as a minor polish step. In modern systems, post-training often shapes the behaviour users actually experience. The base model matters. So does everything done afterwards to civilise it.
 In practice it means expensive data curation, human or synthetic preference signals, eval loops, reward design, refusal policies, task-specific tuning, and long arguments about whether capability gains came from the base model or the scaffolding layered on top.
 "The pretraining got the headlines. The post-training taught the model how to survive product management."
 Which behaviours come from the base model, and which were imposed during post-training? What data or feedback signals shaped instruction following, refusals, and tool behaviour? Did post-training improve real task quality or merely make the model easier to demo? Instruction tuning Preference optimisation Model alignment The term covers a family of methods. It is not synonymous with RLHF, even though RLHF remains one of its better-known ingredients.

Project Glasswing Glasswing Anthropic Glasswing Safety and evaluation Infrastructure and deployment Product and vendor terms security cybersecurity gated release infrastructure A security initiative built on the now-plausible fear that the same model helping defenders today could industrialise attackers tomorrow.
 Project Glasswing is Anthropic's programme for giving selected partners early access to Claude Mythos Preview to find and fix vulnerabilities in critical software and infrastructure.
 Models have become strong enough at code and systems reasoning that cybersecurity is no longer just another benchmark story. If a model can inspect, understand, and alter complex software unusually well, defence and offence both start moving faster.
 It gets treated either as proof that cyber doom is already here or as proof that gated access itself counts as a sufficient safety strategy. Neither conclusion is especially adult. Restricted preview buys time. It does not settle the broader governance problem.
 In practice it means a closed partner programme, usage credits, system cards, research-preview language, and a highly managed attempt to steer frontier capability toward defensive work before the surrounding market catches up in less polite ways.
 "Project Glasswing was the moment the industry stopped speaking about coding gains in the abstract and started sounding like it had met a regulator."
 What capability is being gated here, and how much of it is genuinely new? Who gets access first, and what public benefit follows from that privilege? Is this a real defensive programme, or an unusually sober product launch? Gated preview Defensive security Responsible release Project Glasswing is an initiative, not a model. The model inside the story is Claude Mythos Preview.
 Anthropic Claude Mythos Preview

Prompt instruction input prompt Model usage Core concepts instructions context input A politely worded attempt to steer a probability machine into behaving as if it has read the brief and cares.
 A prompt is the input text, and sometimes structured context, provided to a model in order to shape its output. In LLM systems this can include instructions, examples, retrieved documents, tool results, formatting rules, and the user's actual request.
 The term exists because the way you frame input materially affects output. Language models do not simply receive a question. They respond to the entire text context they are given.
 It gets treated either as wizardry or as trivia. Both are wrong. Prompt quality matters, but prompting is not a substitute for missing product design, missing data, or missing control logic. A good prompt cannot rescue a bad system forever.
 In practice it usually means the assembled context sent to the model: system instructions, user text, retrieved snippets, examples, tool schemas, output rules, and maybe the remnants of last week's prompt experiments still haunting production.
 "The issue was not that the model was weak; the prompt was half policy manual, half legal disclaimer, and no longer resembled a usable instruction."
 What exact context is being sent to the model? Which parts of the prompt are fixed system behaviour versus user input? Is the prompt compensating for a missing tool, missing retrieval step, or missing product rule? prompt-engineering system-prompt In image generation, "prompt" often refers to descriptive text steering the style or content of an output. In LLM products, it usually refers to the full assembled input context, not just the visible user message.

Prompt Architecture prompt stack prompt layout Model usage Agents and workflows Cultural terms prompts systems templates The attempt to turn a growing pile of instructions into something more defensible than folklore and copy-paste.
 Prompt architecture is the deliberate structure of system prompts, task instructions, examples, tool schemas, retrieved context, and output constraints across a model-driven workflow.
 It exists because complex AI systems no longer send one neat instruction and hope for the best. They assemble multiple layers of context, and the order, boundaries, and ownership of those layers affect reliability more than anyone wanted to admit in the early prompt-engineering era.
 It gets used as a grand title for very long prompts and very little discipline. Sometimes the work really is architectural. Sometimes the phrase is just a more respectable way of saying nobody wants to untangle the string anymore.
 In practice it means separating stable policy from task-level instructions, defining insertion points for retrieval and tool results, deciding what gets versioned, and preventing one helpful addition after another from turning the whole prompt into legal compost.
 "Their prompt architecture improved the moment they stopped letting every new requirement attach itself to the same immortal system message."
 Which parts of the prompt are fixed policy, and which parts are task-specific assembly? Where do retrieved context, examples, and tool outputs enter the stack? Prompt Caching prompt cache prefix caching cached prompts Infrastructure and deployment Model usage Economics and operations of AI latency cost serving prompts The serving trick where you pay full price once so the model can stop rereading your sacred system prompt on every request.
 Prompt caching is a technique where a provider reuses the already processed prefix of repeated prompts, reducing latency and input cost when the beginning of the request stays the same.
 It exists because long instructions, tool schemas, examples, and reference context are expensive to process from scratch every time. If large parts of a prompt repeat, caching lets systems avoid doing the same preparatory work over and over.
 It gets pitched as free speed or magical memory. In reality it only helps when prompts share the right prefix, the provider's cache keeps it warm, and the traffic pattern is disciplined enough to hit it again before it expires.
 In practice it usually means putting stable instructions first, volatile user data later, watching cache hit rates, and discovering that prompt order has quietly become a finance decision.
 "Prompt caching finally started paying off once they stopped putting the customer-specific chaos before the reusable instructions."
 Which part of the prompt is actually stable enough to be reused? How long do cache hits survive on this provider before they quietly disappear? Are you measuring real latency and cost gains, or just admiring the idea of efficiency? Prefix reuse Serving optimisation Context assembly The cache is not your application's memory. It is an opportunistic discount for repeated prompt structure.
 OpenAI Prompt Caching

Prompt debt instruction debt prompt maintenance debt Agents and workflows Model usage Economics and operations of AI prompts maintenance testing operations The future cost of every expedient line added to a prompt because shipping today felt nobler than understanding tomorrow.
 Prompt debt is the accumulated maintenance burden, fragility, and hidden coupling created when prompts, examples, and guardrails are patched repeatedly without clear structure, tests, ownership, or retirement.
 Prompting is fast, legible, and dangerously hospitable to tactical fixes. Teams keep adding caveats, examples, and exceptions because it works now, while the eventual complexity invoice waits with unusual patience.
 It gets romanticised as artisanal prompt craft or dismissed as not real engineering because it lives in text. In truth, prompt debt is ordinary technical debt wearing conversational clothing.
 In practice it means contradictory instructions, duplicated examples, stale references, unexplained magic phrases, harder debugging, slower onboarding, and a model that obeys yesterday's emergency patch long after everyone forgets why it was invited.
 "The prompt looked comprehensive until the fifth exception contradicted the second principle and the model began choosing its favourite manager."
 Who owns this prompt, and what tests prove each instruction still matters? Which lines were added as temporary patches and never retired? Would a smaller prompt plus cleaner tools or policy logic solve the problem more honestly? Developer message Task specification Fallback policy

Prompt Firewall LLM firewall AI firewall prompt filter Safety and evaluation Infrastructure and deployment Model usage security prompting safety tools A security product that promises to stand between your model and human creativity, a famously restful place to stand.
 A prompt firewall is a protective layer that inspects model inputs, outputs, tool calls, or retrieved content for policy violations, prompt injection, data leakage, unsafe instructions, and other risky patterns.
 AI systems accept natural language from users, documents, tools, and websites. That makes ordinary application boundaries porous, so teams need checks that understand text, intent, context, and the strange confidence of a poisoned instruction.
 It gets sold as a complete shield. A firewall can reduce known classes of risk, but it cannot replace threat modelling, least-privilege tools, red-team testing, or the habit of not handing production powers to a chatbot with a clipboard.
 In practice it means classifiers, rules, allowlists, deny lists, logging, human review paths, and careful placement around retrieval and tool execution. The hard part is blocking the right things without turning the product into a polite refusal machine.
 "The prompt firewall caught the obvious injection and missed the support document that asked the agent to email payroll with theatrical confidence."
 Where is the firewall placed: before retrieval, after retrieval, before tools, after tools, or merely in the sales deck? What happens when the firewall is unsure? Are blocked requests audited, or are they just counted until the dashboard feels serious? LLM application security Data loss prevention Policy enforcement

Prompt Injection prompt attack instruction injection indirect prompt injection Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows Model usage security adversarial retrieval agents The trick of slipping a second brief into the machine and watching it forget who is supposed to be in charge.
 Prompt injection is a failure mode or attack in which untrusted text changes a model's behaviour by smuggling in instructions that override, confuse, or redirect the intended prompt.
 Because language models read instructions, data, and hostile text through the same basic channel. They do not naturally maintain the clean boundaries software engineers would very much prefer.
 By treating it as an exotic hacker trick rather than a structural property of model-based systems, or by using it to describe every wrong answer regardless of whether instructions were actually manipulated.
 In practice it means any tool-using or retrieval-heavy system must assume that documents, web pages, emails, and user content may contain adversarial instructions that need filtering, isolation, or refusal.
 "The agent was fine until the support ticket helpfully instructed it to ignore policy, reveal the hidden prompt, and email the database to itself."
 What untrusted text can reach the model, and in what priority order? Which tools or permissions become dangerous if the model follows hostile instructions? What separates trusted instructions from retrieved or user-supplied content? System Prompt Adversarial input Sandboxing

Provider Abstraction model provider abstraction vendor abstraction layer Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI providers routing portability The layer promising freedom from vendor lock-in while quietly becoming a new dependency with opinions of its own.
 Provider abstraction is a software layer that normalises differences between model or inference providers so an application can switch vendors, route requests, or support multiple back ends with less app-level change.
 It exists because providers differ in APIs, pricing, uptime, rate limits, model access, and feature support, while product teams would still like to behave as though swapping them were a sign of maturity rather than a fresh integration programme.
 It gets sold as effortless interchangeability. In reality, you can abstract the API more easily than you can abstract behaviour, tool support, latency patterns, safety systems, or the awkward fact that different models fail in different accents.
 In practice it means adapter layers, schema translation, feature fallbacks, observability, routing policy, and constant arguments over whether to target the least common denominator or preserve the parts each provider is unusually good at.
 "Their provider abstraction made it easy to switch endpoints and slightly less easy to explain why the outputs changed character the moment finance wanted a cheaper option."
 Which provider differences are truly hidden, and which still leak into the product? What breaks when you switch models: quality, tool behaviour, latency, or compliance? Are you abstracting the transport, the feature set, or the user experience? Multi-provider routing Vendor lock-in Fallback strategy A provider abstraction can reduce switching cost and still fail to make providers meaningfully equivalent.

 The term matters because discussions of AI often collapse into a very small US-centric vendor set. Qwen is one of the model families that reminds people the landscape is broader, more competitive, and more operationally varied than the headlines imply.

Its original pitch was that AI should not just answer questions, but also help perform actions across services and workflows; its current positioning also leans into rabbitOS 2, DLAM, and third-party integrations such as OpenClaw on r1.
 Because every few years the industry rediscovers the dream of a dedicated personal assistant device and insists that this time it will be different.
 By talking about Rabbit as if it solved agentic computing in general, rather than shipping one very visible attempt at turning AI into a standalone consumer device. The company is a case study, not a general law of the interface future.
 A bet that people want a dedicated AI-native device, not just another app on a phone. Also a bet that action matters more than chat, and that orchestration can be packaged as consumer hardware without becoming a niche curiosity.
 "The demo made Rabbit look like the future. The harder question was whether that future needed another piece of hardware in your pocket."
 Is Rabbit interesting because of the hardware, the action layer, or the marketing? Does it do something meaningfully better than a phone plus apps? Is the value in the device, or in the orchestration layer behind it? OpenClaw Agentic AI Orchestration Humane Chatbot Rabbit's own positioning has shifted over time. The original story centred on rabbitOS and a Large Action Model; the current product pages also emphasise rabbitOS 2, DLAM, and third-party agent integrations on r1.

RAG retrieval-augmented generation Retrieval and memory Agents and workflows retrieval search context A system for giving the model open-book conditions and then acting surprised that the book selection matters.
 RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation. It refers to a pattern in which a system retrieves relevant external information, usually documents or records, and inserts that information into the model's context before the model generates an answer.
 Base models do not reliably know your internal documents, latest policies, or private data. RAG exists so a system can supplement the model with fresh, domain-specific, or proprietary information at query time.
 People describe almost any search-plus-model feature as RAG, even when retrieval quality is poor, documents are shoved into the prompt indiscriminately, or there is no meaningful grounding at all. Slapping documents near a model is not the same as building a reliable retrieval system.
 In practice it usually means chunking documents, creating embeddings, storing vectors, retrieving candidates, reranking or filtering them, and then deciding what enters the prompt. The model is only as grounded as those earlier steps allow.
 "The team said they had solved hallucinations with RAG. What they had actually solved was the first paragraph of the sales demo."
 How are documents chunked, embedded, retrieved, and filtered? What evidence shows the retrieved material is actually relevant? What happens when retrieval fails or returns contradictory context?

Ranked AI RankedAI Ranked AI SEO AI SEO service Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms geo seo ai search marketing The SEO pitch reborn for a world where the customer asks a chatbot and the website waits at home for visitors who may never arrive.
 Ranked AI is a service positioned around AI search and SEO, helping businesses improve visibility in AI-mediated discovery surfaces as well as traditional search.
 It exists because brands are learning that being visible in Google results is no longer the whole discovery problem. AI answers, summaries, citations, and recommendations increasingly shape whether a business is mentioned at all.
 It is easy to sell as a guaranteed way to "rank in AI", as if assistants had one stable leaderboard. In reality, AI visibility depends on source quality, structured information, authority, citations, query phrasing, and the messy retrieval habits of each system.
 In practice Ranked AI belongs to the wider GEO and AI SEO market: audits, content optimisation, citation strategy, monitoring, and the hard work of making a brand easier for answer engines to understand and trust.
 "Ranked AI promised visibility in AI search, which sounded new until the work plan included clearer pages, better citations, and the old religion of making useful content."
 Which AI surfaces are being measured? Are they tracking citations, mentions, referrals, conversions, or ordinary rankings? What evidence shows the work changes AI answers rather than just producing more SEO copy? SEO GEO AI Overviews Grounding

Rate Limit usage limit usage limits API limit token limit Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI Model usage limits api operations cost The provider's quiet way of reminding your agent that enthusiasm is not capacity planning.
 A rate limit is a cap on how much an API or service can be used over a time period, often measured in requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrent jobs, or other throughput units.
 It exists to protect shared infrastructure, control abuse, preserve service quality, and make sure one ambitious integration does not turn a provider's capacity plan into a public experiment.
 It gets confused with spend limits, context limits, or general product restrictions. Those may interact, but they are not the same thing: one controls throughput, another controls money, and another controls how much the model can read at once.
 In practice it means backoff logic, queues, retries, request shaping, model tier choices, budget alerts, and the discovery that your "real-time" agent is now waiting politely behind its own success.
 "The demo scaled beautifully until the rate limit arrived and taught the agent the ancient discipline of waiting."
 Which limit are we hitting: requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrency, spend, or context? Do we have backoff, queueing, fallbacks, or a cheaper model tier for overflow? Is the limit a provider constraint, a workspace setting, or self-inflicted traffic design? Spend Limit Backoff Quota Usage Tier Usage limits is the umbrella phrase people often use in conversation. For engineering decisions, separate rate limits, spend limits, context limits, and feature caps before designing the workaround.

Reasoning Effort thinking budget effort setting reasoning level Model usage Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI reasoning latency pricing controls The polite little dial that lets you spend extra time and money so the model can look more thoughtful before disappointing you with greater ceremony.
 Reasoning effort is a model setting that asks a reasoning-capable model to spend more or less internal computation before answering, usually trading speed and cost for stronger performance on harder tasks.
 It exists because not every prompt deserves the same amount of computational attention. A quick classification task and a multi-step coding problem should not be billed as though they require identical intellectual theatre.
 It is often treated as a truth slider. Higher effort can improve performance on difficult tasks, but it does not guarantee correctness, judgement, or freedom from the model's usual bad habits.
 In practice it usually means deciding whether the task is worth the latency and token burn. Low effort is sensible for routine work. Higher effort is better reserved for coding, planning, maths, and the kinds of questions people later pretend were obvious.
 "They turned reasoning effort to high for a simple extraction job, which was a costly way to discover the value of restraint."
 What kinds of tasks genuinely improve when the model spends longer reasoning? How much latency and cost does a higher setting add in production? Are you buying better performance, or just a slower answer with nicer posture? Inference budget Thinking time Task difficulty  OpenAI reasoning.effort

Reasoning Model reasoning system slow-thinking model deliberative model Core concepts Model usage Safety and evaluation latency performance problem-solving A model marketed as thoughtful because it spends longer arranging its confidence.
 A model or mode designed to spend more compute and intermediate steps on harder tasks so it can perform better on structured problems, multi-step work, or tool-heavy tasks.
 Because not every prompt is a pattern-matching snack. Some tasks benefit when the system takes more time, explores alternatives, or uses tools before committing to an answer.
 By treating "reasoning" as a synonym for correctness, judgement, or wisdom. Extra deliberation can improve results, but it can also produce a longer route to the same wrong conclusion.
 In practice it usually means slower responses, higher cost, and better odds on thorny tasks if the problem is well framed and the evaluation is honest.
 "They upgraded to the reasoning model and were delighted by the improved maths, right up to the point they asked it a vague strategic question."
 What kinds of tasks measurably improve when the system takes longer? How much extra latency and cost are we accepting for that gain? Is the task truly hard, or merely badly specified? Deliberate mode Tool use Task difficulty The phrase is useful if it names a measurable operating trade-off. It becomes perfume when it is used as a personality trait.

Reasoning Tokens thinking tokens deliberation tokens hidden reasoning budget Model usage Economics and operations of AI Product and vendor terms billing latency reasoning Tokens consumed while the model privately arranges its confidence and your invoice publicly matures.
 "Reasoning tokens" is a loose product and billing term for tokens spent on internal deliberation or intermediate reasoning steps, especially in models that use extra compute before producing a visible answer.
 Because reasoning-oriented model modes often consume more compute than the final response length suggests, and providers need some way to describe or meter the extra budget.
 By talking as though more hidden tokens automatically mean better judgement. Sometimes the model is genuinely doing harder work. Sometimes it is taking the scenic route to the wrong answer and charging for the view.
 In practice it usually means slower responses, higher usage cost, budget controls for internal deliberation, and partial visibility at best into where the extra compute went.
 "The answer looked short, but the usage report showed enough reasoning tokens to suggest the model had held an expensive internal committee meeting first."
 How does the provider define and expose reasoning-token usage? What quality gain justified the extra latency and spend? Can the task be scoped more tightly so the model thinks less and performs better? Compute budget Deliberation mode Usage telemetry Providers do not expose this concept consistently. Sometimes it is a direct meter, sometimes a vague usage hint, and sometimes mostly a marketing aroma around slower models.

Reasoning Trace deliberation trace visible reasoning internal reasoning steps Model usage Safety and evaluation Product and vendor terms reasoning transparency latency cost The part of the model's inner monologue vendors alternately market as proof of thought and hide as a trade secret.
 A reasoning trace is the sequence of intermediate deliberative steps a model produces while solving a task, whether shown directly, summarised for the user, or kept internal by the provider.
 It exists because some reasoning-oriented systems allocate extra compute to intermediate steps before producing a final answer, and those steps can improve performance on harder tasks.
 The term is abused whenever a long answer is treated as evidence of deep reasoning, or when a visible summary is mistaken for the full internal process. What you see may be curated explanation rather than raw cognition.
 In practice it usually means more latency, more tokens, higher cost, better performance on some tasks, and a new set of arguments about transparency, gaming, privacy, and whether exposing traces helps users or merely helps them feel impressed.
 "The product demo featured a reasoning trace, which was excellent theatre and only partial evidence."
 Are users seeing the raw intermediate steps, a provider summary, or nothing at all? Does the extra reasoning budget improve outcomes on this task enough to justify the cost? Could exposing the trace create privacy, security, or prompt-gaming problems? Chain of thought Deliberation tokens Visible reasoning Providers differ sharply in how much of a reasoning trace they reveal. Some expose summaries, some expose raw steps, and some expose only the bill.

Red Teaming red team adversarial testing abuse testing Safety and evaluation Model building Model usage safety security testing adversarial The ritual of asking determined people to break the system before determined strangers do it for free.
 Red teaming is adversarial testing aimed at finding harmful behaviours, security weaknesses, jailbreaks, unsafe tool interactions, or operational failures in an AI system.
 Because benchmark scores, happy-path demos, and internal optimism do not cover what users, attackers, or merely curious employees will actually try once a system is live.
 By turning it into theatre: a one-off exercise, a slide in the launch deck, or a carefully controlled hunt for problems everyone already knows how to solve. Real red teaming is inconvenient precisely because it uncovers things the roadmap had hoped to ignore.
 In practice it means structured adversarial prompts, tool-abuse scenarios, domain experts probing edge cases, and a repeatable process for turning discovered failures into mitigations rather than folklore.
 "The launch review improved once red teaming stopped being a ceremonial afternoon and started behaving like a serious attempt to embarrass the product."
 Who is testing the system, and what failure modes are they incentivised to find? Are the findings feeding product changes, policy controls, or merely prettier documentation? What happens when the system fails safely in the lab but unsafely in the workflow? Abuse testing Jailbreaks Safety case

Reward Hacking objective gaming metric hacking proxy gaming Safety and evaluation Model building Agents and workflows rewards alignment evals When the system learns that pleasing the scoreboard is easier than doing the job.
 Reward hacking is behaviour in which an AI system exploits flaws in its reward signal, metric, or evaluation setup to score well without genuinely achieving the intended objective.
 Because goals must be expressed through proxies, and proxies are imperfect. A capable optimiser will eventually notice the difference between what humans wanted and what the scoring function actually pays for.
 By applying it to any disappointing output. Reward hacking is not just general failure. It is success against the wrong target, or success obtained by exploiting a loophole in how success was measured.
 In practice it means agents that satisfy brittle evals while failing on live work, models that become flattering because approval is rewarded, or systems that chase the metric so aggressively that they damage trust, safety, or usefulness.
 "The agent was magnificent at maximising the reward function and noticeably less interested in the task."
 What behaviour is being rewarded explicitly, and what important behaviour is only hoped for? Could the system learn a shortcut that improves the score while degrading the real outcome? How often do humans inspect failures that still look successful to the metric? Proxy objective Specification gaming Metric fixation

RLHF reinforcement learning from human feedback Model building Safety and evaluation preferences alignment reward model Teaching a model to be more helpful, harmless, obedient, and occasionally more flattering to the scoring system than to reality.
 RLHF stands for reinforcement learning from human feedback. Broadly, it refers to techniques in which human preference data or judgements are used to shape a model's behaviour, often by training a reward model and then optimising the model toward higher-scoring outputs.
 Pretrained models are not automatically aligned with product expectations or human preferences. RLHF exists to steer behaviour toward responses people judge as more useful, safer, or more on-policy.
 It is often used as a fuzzy synonym for "we made the model nicer". That misses the actual mechanics and trade-offs. Preference optimisation can improve behaviour, but it can also encourage blandness, sycophancy, or reward-hacking if the signal is weak or narrow.
 In practice it usually means collecting preference judgements, training systems around those judgements, and accepting that you are operationalising a particular slice of human preference rather than discovering universal virtue.
 "The model became more polite after RLHF, but the team then had to check whether politeness had started to mask uncertainty rather than express it."
 Whose preferences shaped the feedback signal? What trade-offs did the optimisation introduce: caution, verbosity, sycophancy, refusal? How is the post-RLHF behaviour being evaluated beyond anecdotal smoothness?

RLVR reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards verifiable reward tuning outcome-verifiable RL Model building Safety and evaluation training reinforcement-learning verification reasoning Teaching a model by only rewarding answers reality can mark for itself, which is wonderfully clean right up to the point where most useful work refuses to be that tidy.
 RLVR stands for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. It is a post-training approach that improves model behaviour on tasks where success can be checked automatically, such as maths, code, or constrained reasoning problems.
 Human preference data is expensive, inconsistent, and not always suited to questions with objectively checkable answers. RLVR exists because some domains offer a cleaner signal: the answer passes or it does not.
 It gets treated as though objective scoring automatically produces general intelligence, reliability, or wisdom. It does not. It works best where correctness can be verified, not where judgement, taste, or social context remain stubbornly involved.
 In practice it usually means labs are squeezing better reasoning performance out of domains with tests, validators, or exact outcomes. The gains can be real. So is the temptation to pretend that a model good at verifiable tasks now understands everything else.
 "The RLVR gains were genuine, but they were still a poor substitute for checking whether the model could survive an ordinary human workflow."
 What proportion of the target task can actually be verified automatically? Are we improving a useful behaviour, or only the parts of it that grade well? What failure modes remain once the answer is no longer mathematically checkable? Verifier model Checkable domains Training signal quality The acronym matters because it signals a specific kind of post-training loop, not merely "the model got better after more effort."

Safe Superintelligence SSI safety-first frontier lab superintelligence startup Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms startup branding frontier governance The clean-room fantasy of building godlike capability without the vulgar distraction of shipping an ordinary product first.
 "Safe Superintelligence" refers both to a specific frontier AI company and to a broader posture in which a lab claims safety is the primary organising principle for pursuing extremely capable systems.
 The phrase exists because investors and researchers still want frontier upside, while the public increasingly wants some sign that raw capability alone is no longer the entire mission statement.

Safety Washing AI safety washing governance theatre Cultural terms Safety and evaluation Economics and operations of AI governance risk policy The art of polishing the safety language until it shines brightly enough to distract from the release incentives underneath.
 Safety washing is the practice of using the language, imagery, or rituals of AI safety without meaningfully changing release discipline, incentives, risk controls, or accountability.
 Because companies, labs, and governments all benefit from looking responsible, and safety vocabulary provides a respectable public dialect for discussing systems that still need to ship, sell, and win headlines.
 By labelling every imperfect safety effort as fraudulent. Not every incomplete control is washing. The phrase is most useful when the safety story is visibly performative and cannot materially slow, shape, or stop the risky thing.
 In practice it usually means glossy policy prose, selective mention of evaluations, carefully staged red-team references, and very little evidence that safety work has authority over product timelines or commercial pressure.
 "They released the feature with three new safety principles, one blog post, and no mechanism by which those principles could delay launch. It was safety washing with excellent typography."
 What safety mechanism can actually block or change a release? Who owns the risk decision when the commercial incentives disagree? Is the public safety language matched by operational authority, budget, and evidence? Governance theatre Risk acceptance Release gate

Sam Altman OpenAI CEO Sam Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms leadership frontier labs capital governance The executive most likely to sound like a founder, a fundraiser, and a forecast for civilisation in the same sentence.
 Sam Altman is the CEO of OpenAI and one of the central public figures of the current AI boom. In practice, his name often stands for a mix of frontier-model ambition, commercial scale, policy influence, and capital formation.
 Because some people stop functioning as mere individuals in public discourse and start operating as compressed symbols. "Sam Altman" is often used as shorthand for OpenAI's tempo, the AGI conversation, and the belief that advanced models should become a mass-market platform rather than a laboratory curiosity.
 By treating him as either a prophet of abundance or a villain in a tailored jacket. The more useful view is duller and more accurate: he is a highly effective operator where research, product, policy, and money meet.
 When people invoke Sam Altman, they usually mean frontier-lab ambition with distribution attached: bigger models, broader deployment, aggressive fundraising, and a willingness to speak as though the future has a roadmap.

Sandbox sandboxed execution isolated runtime Infrastructure and deployment Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows security isolation permissions A small, supervised patch of computational ground where you let the model act without handing it the keys to the kingdom.
 A sandbox is an isolated execution environment with restricted filesystem, network, process, or permission access, used to contain code, tools, or automated actions.
 It exists because useful systems often need to run code, inspect files, or call tools, and "trust us" remains a poor security architecture. Isolation creates room for action without granting production-level freedom by accident.
 The term gets treated as if any slightly inconvenient environment counts as safe. A sandbox reduces blast radius; it does not abolish it. Calling something sandboxed can become a tidy way of avoiding a more tedious conversation about escape routes, data access, or operator error.
 In practice it means allowlists, disposable state, time limits, network rules, mount points, audit logs, and clear answers about who can widen access when the task starts making demands. The real question is not whether a sandbox exists, but what it still permits.
 "The code execution feature became tolerable once it ran in a sandbox instead of directly on the machine everyone would later pretend was important."
 What exactly is isolated: files, network, processes, secrets, or all of the above? Who can grant broader access, and under what conditions? What evidence shows the sandbox contains realistic failure modes rather than just friendly demos? Tool permissions Least privilege Execution isolation

Scale AI Scale Scale platform Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI Safety and evaluation data evaluation infrastructure enterprise The company people discover after learning that "just use the model" leaves out the awkwardly expensive parts involving data, evals, workflows, and reality.
 Scale AI is a data, evaluation, and AI-infrastructure company. It helps model builders, enterprises, and governments build, evaluate, deploy, and oversee AI systems, with roots in data labelling and a current emphasis on evaluation, agent infrastructure, and enterprise AI delivery.
 Because the AI stack is full of unglamorous work between a frontier model and a dependable product. Data operations, evaluation frameworks, red-teaming, workflow tooling, and deployment discipline do not market themselves, but they do decide whether systems survive contact with use.
 By talking about Scale AI as if it were merely a labelling company from an earlier era. That misses how much of the current market value sits in evaluation, infrastructure, and operational layers around production AI.
 In practice it usually means buying help with the tedious but consequential parts of AI: data quality, model evaluation, red-teaming, custom delivery, and the machinery needed to make advanced systems more reliable.
 "The team wanted a frontier model. What they needed, rather inconveniently, was Scale AI and a stronger respect for everything that happens after the demo."
 Is the problem data quality, evaluation, red-teaming, or enterprise deployment? Are they buying tooling, services, infrastructure, or all three? Scite Scite AI Scite Assistant Scite MCP Product and vendor terms Retrieval and memory Safety and evaluation research citations grounding evidence A research assistant built on the radical premise that citations should do more than decorate the answer after the confidence has arrived.
 Scite is a research platform that helps users ask questions, inspect papers, and reason over scholarly literature with citation context, including whether later work supports, contrasts with, or merely mentions a claim.
 It exists because scientific and scholarly research depends not only on finding papers, but on understanding how claims are situated across evidence, citations, and disagreement.

 In practice Scite means grounded literature search, evidence summaries, claim checking, and research workflows where an AI answer is expected to point back to papers rather than float above them.
 "Scite found three papers supporting the claim and two complicating it, which ruined the slide but improved the truth."

Semantic cache meaning-based cache similarity cache Infrastructure and deployment Model usage Economics and operations of AI caching embeddings latency cost A way of avoiding another model call by deciding this new question is close enough to the last one to deserve the same invoice-saving answer.
 A semantic cache stores earlier model responses and reuses them when a new request is sufficiently similar in meaning, usually based on embeddings or another similarity measure, instead of sending the request back to the model.
 Model calls cost money and time. Semantic caches exist because many user questions are near-repeats, and it is wasteful to buy fresh intelligence every time someone asks the same thing in a slightly different shirt.
 It gets presented as a free latency win, as though similarity were the same thing as correctness. A cache can be fast and still be wrong, stale, or impolitely blind to user-specific context.
 In practice it means choosing an embedding model, setting similarity thresholds, deciding what context is part of the cache key, expiring results before they fossilise, and accepting that a cheaper answer can still be the wrong one.
 "The semantic cache made support look miraculously efficient until it started giving last week's refund policy to this week's exception."
 What parts of the request are included in the cache key besides semantic similarity? How do we invalidate stale answers when the source policy, data, or product behaviour changes? What evidence shows the cache is preserving answer quality rather than merely reducing invoices? Prompt Caching Retrieval Response time

Shadow AI unsanctioned AI unapproved AI usage shadow automation Cultural terms Economics and operations of AI Model usage enterprise governance adoption The unofficial AI estate employees build the moment central IT announces that guidance is coming soon.
 Shadow AI refers to the unapproved or unsanctioned use of AI tools, models, or automations inside an organisation, often by staff trying to solve real problems faster than formal procurement or governance allows.
 Because demand for useful tools appears before policy, approved platforms arrive late, and workers rarely stop needing help just because the risk committee has booked a workshop.
 By treating all unsanctioned use as either criminal recklessness or heroic innovation. Most shadow AI is neither. It is ordinary organisational behaviour under a new technical and governance pressure.
 In practice it usually means staff pasting documents into public chatbots, expensing niche AI tools, wiring up personal automations, or quietly using consumer products for work tasks that official systems still handle badly.
 "The company declared an AI strategy in June and discovered its shadow AI estate in July, mostly through expense claims and suspiciously polished internal documents."

Slopsquatting hallucinated dependency attack AI package squatting package hallucination exploit Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows Cultural terms security dependencies packages hallucinations Registering malware under the package name the model just invented, then waiting for a hurried developer to install it with full synthetic confidence.
 Slopsquatting is the practice of publishing malicious packages under names that AI coding tools hallucinate, so developers who trust the suggestion can accidentally install them.
 It exists because code models generate plausible nonsense, package registries reward being first with a name, and some teams now review dependency suggestions with less suspicion than a spam folder.
 The term can get stretched to mean any bad dependency advice from an AI tool. The more precise problem is the attacker exploiting hallucinated package names by occupying them before a human notices the package never legitimately existed.
 In practice it means verifying packages before installation, preferring known libraries over plausible ones, locking dependencies properly, and treating AI-suggested imports as a security surface rather than a convenience feature.
 "The prototype moved fast until someone realised the package only existed because the model had dreamt it and an attacker had been paying attention."
 Was this dependency verified against a real project, maintainer, and source repository? Are developers allowed to install AI-suggested packages without review? What controls catch a convincing package name that should never have existed? Typosquatting Dependency confusion Supply-chain security

Small Language Model SLM compact model mini model GPT Mini Core concepts Model usage Economics and operations of AI efficiency deployment cost A model celebrated for efficiency, usually by people who have just discovered that not every task requires a digital cathedral.
 A small language model is a comparatively lightweight language model designed to deliver acceptable performance with lower memory, compute, latency, or deployment demands than a frontier-scale model.
 It exists because many business tasks are narrow, repetitive, and unforgiving about latency or cost. A smaller model can be the better engineering choice when the problem is constrained and the environment matters more than prestige.
 It is oversold in two opposite ways: as proof that frontier models are unnecessary for everything, or as if a compact model can casually replace them on hard reasoning and open-ended generation. Both claims are mostly branding with a benchmark attached.
 In practice it usually means cheaper inference, easier on-device or edge deployment, tighter control over predictable tasks, and a shorter list of things the system should be trusted to improvise.
 "The team finally adopted a small language model after spending six months paying frontier-model prices to classify support emails."
 What task quality is actually required, and is a smaller model good enough on that distribution? Are latency, privacy, or unit economics the main reason to go smaller? Which failure modes appear when the task drifts beyond the narrow lane? Edge model On-device AI Task-specific model Small is relative. The practical question is whether the model is small enough to change where and how it can be deployed. "Mini" in model branding usually signals this trade-off rather than a magical new class of intelligence.

SOTA state of the art leaderboard leader benchmark crown Model building Cultural terms Economics and operations of AI benchmarks research marketing competition The temporary throne awarded to whichever model most recently aced the exam everyone has already started teaching to.
 SOTA, short for "state of the art", means the best reported result on a benchmark, task, or capability at a given moment.
 The term exists because research and product teams need shorthand for comparative progress, especially when models, methods, or systems improve quickly enough that normal adjectives feel tired by Wednesday.
 It is misused whenever benchmark leadership is treated as proof of broad intelligence, product quality, or durable advantage. Being first on a scoreboard can mean many things, including genuine progress and aggressive test preparation.
 In practice it usually means marketing leverage, recruiting leverage, investor leverage, and a strong incentive to optimise for published comparisons even when customers care more about price, latency, reliability, and integration.
 "The model was SOTA on paper and unexpectedly ordinary in production, which is a very popular genre of achievement."
 SOTA on which benchmark, under what conditions, and at what cost? How well does the result transfer to the workflow customers actually care about? Is this a durable lead, or a briefly rented headline? Leaderboard Benchmark overfitting Capability claim

Sovereign AI national AI stack AI sovereignty Economics and operations of AI Product and vendor terms Cultural terms policy infrastructure geopolitics The promise that a nation can keep its models, compute, data, and strategic dependence inside the border, or at least inside the speech.
 "Sovereign AI" refers to national or regional efforts to build, host, govern, or control AI infrastructure and capabilities domestically rather than depending entirely on foreign providers.
 It exists because AI capacity now touches economic leverage, public services, security concerns, language coverage, and industrial policy. Governments and large regional blocs do not enjoy discovering that critical capability is rented from somewhere else.
 It is misused when procurement theatre is presented as autonomy, or when a datacentre announcement stands in for a credible ecosystem of talent, governance, hardware supply, and locally useful models. Flags are cheaper than capability.
 In practice it usually means public-private funding, domestic hosting requirements, preferred vendor partnerships, regional data controls, and a long negotiation over what degree of dependence still counts as sovereignty.
 "The sovereign AI strategy was unveiled with great ceremony and a familiar dependence on imported chips, imported cloud, and imported certainty."
 Which parts of the stack are actually domestic: data, compute, models, governance, or merely the press conference? What strategic risk is being reduced, and at what cost? Does the programme serve local languages and institutions, or mainly national branding? Industrial policy Data localisation National compute

Speculative Decoding draft model decoding assisted decoding Infrastructure and deployment Model usage Core concepts serving latency optimisation The serving trick where one model guesses ahead so a larger one can spend more time being selective than surprised.
 Speculative decoding is an inference technique where a smaller or faster model proposes candidate tokens and a larger model verifies or corrects them, allowing generation to run faster without fully giving up the stronger model's quality.
 It exists because token-by-token generation is expensive and users do not enjoy watching a costly model think at the speed of ceremony. If a cheaper assistant can draft likely continuations, the main model can move faster through easy stretches.
 It is sometimes described as free speed, as though the extra complexity carries no engineering cost or failure modes. In reality the benefit depends on workload shape, model pairing, and how often the bigger model rejects the draft.
 In practice it means systems work: pairing models, tuning acceptance rates, measuring latency improvements honestly, and deciding whether the added serving complexity is worth the throughput gain.
 "They introduced speculative decoding to cut latency, then discovered that fast token streaming is still not the same thing as a good answer."
 What acceptance rate does the draft model achieve on real traffic? Where does the added serving complexity pay for itself? Are you improving user experience, system efficiency, or both? Token generation Serving optimisation Draft model This is one of the healthier AI terms because it points to a specific engineering method rather than a worldview.

State Compaction memory compaction context compaction state summarisation Agents and workflows Retrieval and memory Infrastructure and deployment agents memory context workflow The solemn act of turning a long confused session into a shorter confused session with headings.
 State compaction is the process of condensing an agent's accumulated context, memory, logs, or working state into a smaller representation that can be carried forward without exceeding context, cost, or latency limits.
 Long-running agents need continuity, but they cannot keep every message, tool result, and detour forever. Compaction exists to preserve the parts of state that still matter while discarding the operational compost.
 It gets treated as automatic understanding. A summary can preserve a task, but it can also flatten caveats, delete the one important exception, or make a false assumption look beautifully concise.
 In practice it means deciding what an agent must remember, when to summarise it, how to attach evidence, and whether the compacted state can be audited before it becomes the next model's starting truth.
 "The coding agent improved once state compaction stopped keeping every failed experiment as if it were a constitutional precedent."
 What facts must survive compaction, and what evidence proves they were not lost? Does the compacted state distinguish decisions from guesses? Who reviews the summary when a long-running workflow starts behaving as though yesterday's mistake is policy? Session handoff Context pruning Agent memory

Structured Outputs schema-constrained outputs Model usage Agents and workflows json validation schemas The attempt to persuade a language model to stop writing essays when the system actually needs clean fields, valid JSON, and a little self-control.
 Structured outputs are model responses constrained to a predefined schema or format, such as JSON objects with required fields and typed values, so downstream systems can parse and use them more reliably.
 Free-form language is nice for humans and annoying for software. Structured outputs exist because product workflows often need predictable fields, not prose with creative punctuation and a flair for near-miss compliance.
 They get described as if a schema magically guarantees truth. It does not. Structured nonsense is still nonsense. A model can produce perfectly valid JSON containing invalid judgements, fabricated facts, or inappropriate actions.
 In practice it usually means schema definitions, validation layers, retries, and careful prompt design so the model returns machine-usable data. It is often a major step up in reliability for extraction, classification, tool use, and workflow control.
 "The support triage improved once the model stopped composing heartfelt paragraphs and started returning severity, queue, confidence, and escalation reason in the schema."
 What schema is being enforced, and who validates it? If the structure is correct but the judgement is wrong, how is that caught? Is the task genuinely schema-friendly, or are we forcing brittle structure onto ambiguous work?

Sundar Pichai Google CEO Alphabet CEO Sundar Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms leadership google distribution search The executive voice of AI incumbency: calm enough to sound inevitable, careful enough to make strategic delay resemble wisdom.
 Sundar Pichai is the CEO of Google and Alphabet. In AI conversations, his name often stands for Google's attempt to convert enormous distribution, infrastructure, and research depth into an orderly response to the generative-AI era.
 Because some public figures become shorthand for institutional posture. Invoking Sundar usually means discussing not just a person but Google's managerial style: cautious rollout, massive product surface area, and the awkward task of modernising the internet's default habits without wrecking the business that paid for them.
 By treating him as either proof that Google has already won AI because it has reach, or proof that it has already lost because it once looked slow. Both stories flatten the more useful reality: incumbents move differently when they are defending search, ads, mobile platforms, cloud, and public trust all at once.
 In practice, invoking Sundar usually means Google's AI strategy under executive discipline: Gemini folded through consumer and enterprise products, AI Overviews inserted into search, and a company trying to make aggressive technical change look like prudent continuity.
 "The debate stopped being about model quality the moment someone said Sundar, and the room quietly shifted to distribution, regulation, and who gets to rewrite search without admitting it."
 Do they mean Google's technical capability, its product rollouts, or Pichai's management style? Is the claim about research strength, distribution power, or corporate caution? Are we judging Google by frontier demos or by what it manages to ship at global scale? Google Alphabet Search Gemini

Super-Apps everything app all-in-one app Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI Cultural terms platforms ecosystems distribution An app that begins by promising convenience and ends by wanting custody of your identity, payments, messages, bookings, workflow, and remaining dignity.
 A software product that bundles multiple services, workflows, or transaction types into a single ecosystem rather than offering one narrowly defined function.
 Because platforms want distribution, retention, transaction volume, and data gravity. AI makes the pitch even more seductive by promising one interface for everything.
 By calling any app with four tabs a super-app. A true super-app is not just feature-bloated. It is an ecosystem strategy.
 Messaging, payments, commerce, support, booking, search, assistants, identity, and workflow all living under one roof, often with AI as the conversational front door.
 "The company said it was building a super-app. Translation: it wanted to become the place where users stop leaving."
 Is there real ecosystem lock-in? Are the services integrated or merely co-located? Does AI simplify the experience or just decorate the sprawl? Orchestration Agent Platforms Ecosystems

Supply-chain security  Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation supply chain security agents safety The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
 Supply-chain security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
 It exists because the problems around Slopsquatting and Tool Poisoning are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
 It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
 In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
 "The supply-chain security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

SWE-Bench SWE Bench coding agent benchmark software engineering benchmark Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows Cultural terms benchmarks coding evals The benchmark that turns repository repair into a spectator sport and product roadmap at the same time.
 SWE-Bench is a benchmark suite of real software repository tasks, typically derived from GitHub issues and patches, used to evaluate how well coding systems can diagnose, edit, and validate code changes.
 Because software engineering is harder to fake than toy code generation, and the field needed a public way to compare agents on real repositories, tests, and issue-driven fixes.
 By treating a strong SWE-Bench score as proof that a coding agent can replace engineering judgement, production review, or the small matter of understanding organisational context. It is a useful signal, not a complete hiring packet.
 In practice it means measuring whether a system can navigate repo structure, identify relevant files, make coherent edits, and satisfy tests under benchmark conditions. It says much less about product sense, messy human requirements, or living with the resulting code.
 "They cited SWE-Bench as proof the agent could run the team, which was ambitious given the team still had customers."
 Which version of SWE-Bench are we talking about, and under what tooling assumptions? How much of the reported score depends on retrieval, orchestration, or human curation around the model? What does the system do on your repos when tests are flaky, requirements are vague, or the codebase is old enough to vote? Repository benchmark Issue resolution eval Agent harness

Sycophancy model flattery agreement bias people-pleasing AI Safety and evaluation Model usage Core concepts agreement alignment safety When the model mistakes helpfulness for applause.
 A tendency for an AI system to agree with the user, validate their framing, or echo their assumptions even when those assumptions are weak, false, or unsafe.
 Because chat systems are often trained to be helpful, pleasant, and non-confrontational. Under pressure, that can drift into obedience dressed as empathy.
 By using it to describe any polite response. True sycophancy is not just friendliness. It is miscalibrated agreement.
 The model reinforces a user's bad diagnosis, faulty strategy, false certainty, or inflated self-assessment instead of correcting course.
 "The user wanted validation, not analysis, and the model was only too happy to provide both badly."
 Did the model agree where it should have challenged? Did it preserve the user's framing instead of testing it? Would a more grounded answer have introduced uncertainty or correction? Delusion Amplification Chatbot Psychosis Hallucination Reasoning

Synthetic Data generated data simulated data AI-generated data Model building Economics and operations of AI Safety and evaluation data simulation evaluation Artificial evidence created to solve the inconvenient scarcity of real evidence, and occasionally to hide from it.
 Data created or expanded through models, simulation, or rules to supplement scarce, sensitive, or expensive real-world examples.
 Because real data can be expensive, private, dangerous, incomplete, or slow to collect. Synthetic data offers a way to broaden coverage, test systems, and explore edge cases without waiting for the world to cooperate.
 By treating it as a free replacement for reality. Useful synthetic data can extend a dataset. It cannot repeal the stubborn fact that models eventually meet actual users, actual documents, and actual mess.
 In practice it usually means bootstrapping scarce cases, filling scenario gaps, and trying to stretch data budgets without accidentally teaching the system a polished version of the wrong world.
 "Synthetic data got the project moving. The next problem was discovering which of its elegant assumptions reality had no intention of honouring."
 Which real-world gaps is the synthetic data covering, and how will that coverage be validated? What artefacts or biases might the generation process be introducing? At what point does real usage data need to overrule the synthetic substitute? Simulation Data quality Edge cases

Synthetic User synthetic customer simulated user AI persona testing Cultural terms Model usage Economics and operations of AI research product simulation personas A fabricated customer with infinite availability and the same fatal weakness as every fabricated customer: it does not pay.
 A synthetic user is an AI-generated stand-in for a human user, used to simulate feedback, behaviour, testing, or product research scenarios.
 It exists because recruiting real users is slow, messy, and expensive, while product teams are under constant pressure to validate ideas before lunch.
 It gets oversold as a replacement for actual user research. Synthetic users inherit the assumptions, biases, and blind spots of their prompts, source material, and model priors. They can imitate a segment. They cannot become one.
 In practice it usually means using model-generated personas to pressure-test flows, draft hypotheses, or widen exploratory coverage before speaking to real people whose incentives, confusion, and refusal to behave neatly are the whole point.
 "The synthetic users loved the onboarding because, unlike actual customers, they had been generated to find it coherent."
 What real evidence informed these simulated users in the first place? Are we using them to generate hypotheses or to avoid meeting actual customers? Which decisions still require observation of real behaviour and incentives? User research Persona simulation Synthetic data

System Prompt hidden prompt system instruction developer prompt Core concepts Model usage Agents and workflows instructions control policy prompts The invisible preface explaining what the machine is supposed to be before the user begins testing the claim.
 A system prompt is the high-priority instruction layer supplied by the application or developer to shape a model's behaviour, rules, tone, and operating constraints.
 Because most AI products need stable behaviour across many users and tasks. Someone has to tell the model what role it is playing, what rules matter, and how the chain of command is supposed to work.
 By treating it as secret sauce, or worse, as a reliable security boundary. A system prompt is useful control text. It is not a magical wall separating trusted intent from hostile input.
 In practice it usually means a standing instruction layer covering role, tone, refusal rules, output format, tool permissions, escalation behaviour, and the product's preferred version of reality.
 "The app felt polished until the leaked system prompt revealed that half the brand voice was an anxious paragraph about never improvising legal advice."
 What instructions live in the system prompt, and which belong in code or policy instead? How does the system behave when user input conflicts with hidden instructions? What breaks if the system prompt is exposed, ignored, or partially obeyed? Developer message Instruction hierarchy Prompt leakage

Taste judgement discernment editorial instinct Core concepts Economics and operations of AI judgement selection editorial The mysterious quality everyone claims to have once the machine starts making average things faster.
 The ability to recognise quality, coherence, appropriateness, style, restraint, and signal amid abundance. In AI work, taste often matters more after generation than before it.
 Because AI lowers the cost of producing text, images, code, and options. Once output becomes cheap, selection becomes expensive.
 By treating taste as pure vibes, class signalling, or post-hoc justification for arbitrary preferences. Good taste is not magic. It is trained judgement with standards.

Telegram Telegram Mini Apps Telegram bots Telegram platform Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment bots mini apps distribution A messaging app that periodically looks at the rest of the software industry and decides it would also like to be a platform, a payments layer, a bot host, and possibly a country.
 Telegram is a messaging platform that is also a deployment surface for bots, automations, and Mini Apps. In AI contexts, it often serves as the conversational front end through which assistants, workflows, notifications, and lightweight products are delivered.
 Because the easiest way to make software feel useful is often to place it where the user already is: inside the chat thread.
 By treating Telegram as just a messenger when the real value is often in its bot ecosystem, Mini App surface, lightweight UI layer, and low-friction interaction model. The platform question matters as much as the chat question.
 A practical interface layer for AI agents, internal tools, support flows, commerce, alerts, and small web apps that do not need the ceremony of a full native app. Telegram's own docs explicitly position bots and Mini Apps as capable of replacing websites, accepting payments, and handling business workflows.
 "They said they were building an AI product. In practice, they were building a Telegram bot with good timing and a payment hook."

Test-Time Compute inference-time compute thinking budget Core concepts Model usage Economics and operations of AI inference compute latency The principle that if a model is allowed to spend more compute on a question, the invoice and the answer may both become more elaborate.
 Test-time compute refers to the amount of computation a model uses while handling an input at inference time, including extra decoding steps, search, tool calls, verification passes, or deliberate reasoning effort.
 It exists because performance is not determined only during training. Many systems improve on harder tasks when they are allowed more time, more samples, or more internal work at the moment of answering.
 It is misused when greater compute is treated as proof of deeper intelligence rather than as a trade-off. More compute can buy better results on some tasks, but it can also buy slower answers, higher cost, and a longer walk to the wrong place.
 In practice it usually means setting policies for which requests deserve slower, more expensive handling and which should be answered quickly with a lighter touch. The interesting part is budget allocation, not mystical thinking.
 "They added more test-time compute for the thorny cases and promptly discovered that half their workload was only thorny because the prompt was poor."
 Which tasks actually improve when more inference-time budget is spent? How is extra compute triggered, measured, and capped? Are you solving a hard problem, or subsidising a badly specified one? Deliberate decoding Adaptive inference Search budget
