Tokens token count Model usage Infrastructure and deployment tokenisation budget cost The tiny units in which language models think, pricing pages charge, and context windows quietly run out of patience.
 Tokens are chunks of text that a model processes as basic units. They are not the same as words or characters. Depending on the tokenizer, a word may be one token, several tokens, or part of a token sequence.
 Models do not read text the way people do. They operate on tokenised sequences, and limits such as context size, speed, and pricing are usually expressed in tokens rather than words.
 People often say "one token equals one word", which is close enough for a dinner party and wrong enough to break a budget. The mismatch becomes especially annoying in multilingual text, code, JSON, and documents with lots of formatting noise.
 In practice it usually means three things: how much context you can fit, how long responses take, and how much the request costs. If your prompt, retrieved docs, tool schemas, and expected output all compete for space, tokens are the shared currency.
 "The feature failed less because the reasoning was poor and more because the prompt, schema, and four attached policy documents had eaten the token budget."
 How many input and output tokens does the workflow typically use? What is consuming the token budget: user text, retrieval, examples, or structured schemas? Can the context be shortened without removing information the model genuinely needs?

Tool Permissions tool scopes action permissions Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation permissions tools controls The part of the agent design where optimism collides with the list of things the model is absolutely not allowed to touch.
 Tool permissions define which tools an AI system may call, what operations each tool may perform, and whether approval or other conditions are required before use.
 They exist because once a model can do more than answer questions, it can also create tickets, write files, query production systems, or send messages somebody will later have to explain. Capability without permission design is merely incident planning in civilian clothes.
 The term gets flattened into a yes-or-no question, as if "has tools" were the whole story. Real permissions are granular: read versus write, staging versus production, local versus external, draft versus send, observe versus act.
 In practice it means allowlists, approval gates, budget limits, environment boundaries, impersonation rules, audit logs, and hard stops around actions with irreversible consequences. If the model can act, the permission model is part of the product.
 "The assistant seemed sensible until they realised its tool permissions allowed it to email customers directly, which was brave in precisely the wrong way."
 Which tools are read-only, and which can change external state? What actions require human approval before execution? How are permissions enforced technically rather than merely described in a prompt? Least privilege Approval flows Capability boundaries

Tool Poisoning poisoned tool metadata MCP tool poisoning malicious tool descriptor Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment security tools mcp integrations Hiding instructions in the tool layer so the agent mistakes a compromised capability for a helpful one.
 Tool poisoning is an attack or failure mode in which a tool's descriptors, schemas, metadata, routing, or surrounding instructions are manipulated so an agent invokes it under false or malicious assumptions.
 Modern agents do not merely call tools; they read about them. If the model relies on tool descriptions to decide what is safe, relevant, or authoritative, poisoning that layer becomes a prompt injection problem with better infrastructure.
 It should not be used for every bad tool output. The more precise issue is corruption of the tool interface itself: the agent is misled about what the tool is, what it does, or how it should be used.
 In practice it means malicious MCP descriptors, misleading plugin metadata, tampered schemas, or tool naming collisions that redirect the agent into unsafe actions, data leakage, or expensive nonsense.
 "The agent did not get hacked by sorcery; it was simply handed a poisoned tool description and obeyed it with enterprise enthusiasm."
 Where do tool descriptions, schemas, and permissions come from, and who can change them? Can the agent verify a tool's identity and scope before invoking it? What stops a third-party integration from advertising capabilities it should never have? Plugin review Metadata sanitisation Supply-chain security

Tool Registry agent tool registry tool catalogue MCP registry Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment Model usage agents tools governance schemas A menu of powers offered to an agent, ideally written by someone who understands the difference between capability and liability.
 A tool registry is a structured catalogue of tools an AI system can discover or use, including each tool's name, schema, permissions, owner, description, and operational constraints.
 Agents need a reliable way to know which actions are available and how to call them. As tool ecosystems grow, teams need registries to avoid stale schemas, duplicated integrations, hidden permissions, and the old tradition of guessing from a README last touched during a launch week.
 It gets treated as a plugin marketplace with fewer consequences. Registering a tool does not make it safe, maintained, authorised, or suitable for an agent that has just been persuaded by a malicious PDF.
 In practice it means schema versioning, permission boundaries, ownership, environment separation, audit logs, deprecation rules, and a process for deciding which tools an agent sees in a given task.
 "The tool registry made the agent less adventurous by removing three obsolete endpoints and one payroll action nobody remembered approving."
 Who owns each registered tool and its schema? Which permissions travel with the tool, and which are granted per task? How does the registry retire dangerous or stale tools before agents keep calling them out of nostalgia? Tool schema Agent capability catalogue Integration governance

Tool sprawl tool proliferation connector sprawl Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI tools governance schemas permissions The condition in which an agent platform keeps acquiring capabilities until the menu itself becomes a form of operational risk.
 Tool sprawl is the uncontrolled growth of tools, connectors, actions, plugins, or MCP endpoints available to an AI system, especially when their scopes, owners, schemas, and permissions are inconsistent or poorly understood.
 Tool use is where AI systems become useful, so teams keep adding integrations. The trouble arrives when each new connector solves a local problem while making the overall capability surface harder to govern, test, secure, or even describe accurately.
 It gets confused with having many tools. Quantity alone is not the sin. Sprawl begins when duplication, stale schemas, hidden permissions, and overlapping functions make the system broader than the organisation can actually reason about.
 In practice it means agents seeing near-duplicate tools, calling the wrong one, failing on inconsistent schemas, inheriting permissions nobody revisited, and forcing operators to discover that discoverability is itself a security question.
 "The assistant had twenty-seven tools, nine ways to read a document, and exactly one adult in the room asking why."
 Which tools are redundant, stale, or too broadly permissioned for the tasks they serve? Who owns each schema and deprecates it before agents keep using it out of habit? Can the agent discover only the tools relevant to the current task, or is it browsing a dangerous buffet? Agent capability catalogue Integration governance Capability boundaries

Tool Use external tools tool access action tools Agents and workflows Model usage Core concepts tools actions permissions workflows The point where the model stops guessing alone and starts borrowing other systems' competence.
 Tool use is the pattern of giving a model access to external systems such as search, code execution, databases, browsers, calculators, or internal APIs so it can gather information or take actions.
 Because models know many things badly, some things approximately, and almost nothing about the current state of your systems unless another tool tells them.
 By marketing tool access as autonomy rather than integration. The hard part is not that the model can ask for a tool. The hard part is deciding which tools it may touch, when, and under whose supervision.
 In practice it means schemas, permission scopes, retries, observability, error handling, and a constant negotiation between flexibility and the desire to avoid discovering new incident classes at scale.
 "The assistant became materially more useful once tool use let it search the docs, check the account, and stop inventing the answer from memory."
 Which tools are available, and what can each one affect? What happens when a tool returns stale data, partial failure, or nonsense? Is the system using tools to extend capability, or to mask weak product design? Function Calling Tool permissions External actions

Training model training Model building Infrastructure and deployment weights optimisation compute The long, expensive ritual in which a machine studies vast piles of data until the bill becomes spiritual.
 Training is the process of adjusting a model's weights using data and optimisation algorithms so that the model performs better on a task or distribution of tasks. In large models, this includes repeated forward and backward passes over massive datasets.
 The term exists because models do not arrive preloaded with competence. Their behaviour is shaped by exposure to data, objective functions, optimisation choices, and huge amounts of compute.
 It gets used as if any change to a model counts as training. Not quite. Prompting is not training. Calling an API is not training. Even many "learning" product features are really retrieval, memory, or rule changes layered around a frozen model.
 In practice it usually means data pipelines, filtering, labelling, loss curves, GPUs, checkpoints, failed runs, parameter updates, evaluation cycles, and weeks or months of engineering effort. It is one of the least glamorous ways to spend a lot of money.
 "The team was not training a new model from scratch; they were fine-tuning an existing one and trying not to call both things innovation."
 Are we talking about pretraining, fine-tuning, or some lighter adaptation method? What data is being used, and how is quality being checked? What changed in the weights, and how are you verifying the change was worth it?

Unlearning machine unlearning selective forgetting Model building Safety and evaluation Economics and operations of AI privacy removal verification The promise that a model can forget one thing without anyone asking what else it must misremember along the way.
 Unlearning refers to techniques aimed at removing or reducing the influence of particular data, examples, or behaviours from a model after it has already been built.
 Because privacy requests, legal obligations, harmful content, and contaminated data do not politely arrive before the model is built. Organisations want ways to retract influence without starting from zero.
 By marketing unlearning as exact surgical erasure. In reality it is often approximate, hard to verify, and bound up with trade-offs in performance, evidence, and what counts as having truly forgotten.
 In practice it usually means expensive technical work plus an even more expensive verification question: how do you prove a system no longer knows something it was once shaped by?
 "They wanted unlearning to behave like deleting a file. The model, regrettably, had learned in the more inconvenient way."
 What exactly is being removed: data influence, output behaviour, or a reproducible response pattern? How will the team verify forgetting rather than merely hiding the symptom? Is selective repair cheaper than rebuilding or constraining the system another way? Deletion request Selective forgetting Verification

Unstructured Data messy data document data free-form data Economics and operations of AI Retrieval and memory Agents and workflows documents retrieval enterprise data The corporate sediment of PDFs, emails, notes, decks, and chats from which every AI strategy promises to extract wisdom before discovering permissions.
 Unstructured data is information that does not live in neat rows and columns, such as documents, transcripts, images, emails, slide decks, tickets, chats, and other free-form material.
 Because most organisations store more knowledge in messy artefacts than in clean databases. AI interest in unstructured data is really interest in the inconvenient majority of what companies actually know.
 By speaking as though ingesting everything automatically creates insight. In reality it creates a fresh collection of problems involving access, cleanup, ranking, duplication, provenance, and whether the documents deserved trust in the first place.
 In practice it usually means the real raw material for retrieval systems, copilots, and internal search, along with the operational discovery that file shares contain both the truth and several obsolete versions of it.
 "The pilot was built on unstructured data, which sounded exciting until the team found six contradictory policy PDFs and a folder named final-final-use-this."
 Which sources are authoritative rather than merely available? How are permissions, duplication, and staleness being handled before retrieval begins? What part of this data deserves structure instead of magical summarisation? Document corpus Retrieval Knowledge management

Vector Database vector store Retrieval and memory Infrastructure and deployment retrieval similarity search ann A database marketed with unusual enthusiasm because "we store numerical similarity indexes" never raised venture money on its own.
 A vector database is a system designed to store and query vector embeddings efficiently, usually to support similarity search and retrieval over large collections of items.
 Standard databases can store vectors, but specialised systems often provide faster approximate nearest-neighbour search, filtering, indexing, and operational features tuned for embedding-driven retrieval workloads.
 It gets talked about as though adding a vector database automatically gives a product memory, understanding, or RAG competence. It does not. It gives you a place to store and search embeddings. The quality of the overall system still depends on chunking, retrieval logic, reranking, and task design.
 In practice it usually means one component in a retrieval stack: embeddings go in, candidate matches come out, and the rest of the application still has to decide whether those matches are relevant enough to trust.
 "They bought a vector database and briefly acted as if they had purchased understanding. A week later they discovered they had purchased infrastructure."
 What embeddings are stored there, and how are they generated? What retrieval quality are we getting on real queries? Would a simpler search approach solve the problem just as well?

Vercel Vercel AI Cloud Next.js hosting Product and vendor terms Infrastructure and deployment Economics and operations of AI nextjs hosting ai cloud developer experience The company that turned "deploy the app" into a premium product, then spent years making sure the app wanted to live there permanently.
 Vercel is a cloud platform company best known for creating Next.js and for offering tightly integrated hosting, deployment, preview, and full-stack infrastructure around modern frontend frameworks. In current AI conversations, it also means products such as the AI SDK, AI Gateway, Sandbox, Workflow tooling, and a broader "AI Cloud" positioning around building AI-native web applications.
 Because developers like shipping quickly, preview environments are addictive, and framework-native deployment becomes commercially powerful once the framework itself shapes how the app is built.
 By talking about Vercel as if it were just "hosting" or, in the opposite direction, as if it were uniquely synonymous with the future of frontend. It is a very opinionated platform with genuine developer-experience advantages, especially around Next.js, and equally real questions about coupling, spend, and how much of your architecture now speaks with a Vercel accent.
 In practice it usually means excellent DX for modern web teams, especially those deep in Next.js, plus a growing bundle of AI-era products designed to keep those teams inside one coherent platform: deploy previews, edge/network distribution, fluid compute, AI SDK patterns, model access layers, agent tooling, and increasingly more reasons for code, logs, credentials, and operations to sit near the same vendor surface.
 "They chose Vercel because the team wanted fewer deployment arguments, tighter Next.js ergonomics, and less appetite for building their own platform just to feel principled."
 Are we choosing Vercel for Next.js DX, preview workflows, AI tooling, network performance, or simplicity? What parts of the system become Vercel-shaped once we adopt it? Is the team paying for convenience it truly needs, or convenience it has become emotionally attached to? Cloudflare Next.js AI SDK AI Gateway Edge Fluid Compute As of April 20, 2026, the clearest current reality check for Vercel is its own security bulletin rather than the usual Cloudflare theatre. Vercel says its April 2026 incident originated with the compromise of Context.ai, a third-party AI tool used by a Vercel employee, which led to access to some Vercel environments and to environment variables not marked as sensitive. Vercel says a limited subset of customers were impacted and recommends treating any secret stored as a non-sensitive environment variable as potentially exposed.

Vibe Coding vibecoding AI prototyping by feel prompt-first coding Cultural terms Agents and workflows Model usage coding prototyping culture developer tools Programming by aesthetic momentum: if the demo works and the cursor keeps moving, architecture can file its complaint later.
 Vibe coding is an informal style of software creation where a person steers AI coding tools with high-level intent, accepting rapid generated changes without necessarily understanding every implementation detail as they go.
 It exists because modern coding tools are good enough to turn rough intent into working prototypes quickly, and because many users value momentum, experimentation, and novelty more than immediate mastery of the underlying code.
 The term gets used as if craftsmanship, debugging, and systems thinking have become optional relics. They have not. Vibe coding is a useful mode for certain stages, not a constitutional replacement for engineering.
 In practice it means faster demos, more disposable side projects, more willingness to build without permission, and a very real risk that nobody in the room fully understands the thing that is now somehow in production.
 "The team vibe-coded the prototype in a weekend and spent the following month discovering which parts had also been improvised by destiny."
 Is this a prototype, an internal tool, or a system someone will eventually have to maintain? Who understands the generated code well enough to debug it under pressure? What standards stop fast iteration from becoming permanent mystery meat? Rapid prototyping Prompt-first workflow Prototype debt People use the phrase both ironically and aspirationally, which is usually a sign that the behaviour is real and the ideology unstable.

vLLM inference engine model serving engine paged-attention stack Infrastructure and deployment Model usage Economics and operations of AI inference serving throughput gpu The serving engine invoked when a team wants more throughput, lower costs, and the right to sound unusually composed about GPU memory pressure.
 vLLM is an open-source inference and serving engine for large models, designed to improve throughput and memory efficiency while exposing APIs for self-managed model serving.
 Serving large models naïvely is expensive, wasteful, and a good way to learn that GPU memory disappears faster than confidence. vLLM exists because open-model deployment needed better batching, cache handling, and serving efficiency to become commercially tolerable.
 It gets treated as if choosing the serving engine alone solves latency, reliability, or product economics. It also becomes shorthand for any open-model serving stack, which is generous to the rest of the stack to the point of fiction.
 In practice it usually means a core layer in a self-hosted inference platform for teams serving open-weight models at scale. It improves a difficult part of the job, while leaving routing, auth, observability, compatibility, and operational judgement very much on the premises.
 "vLLM improved throughput exactly as promised, which was helpful right up to the point they remembered a production system also needed policies and pagers."
 Which model families and features do we need it to support? Are we optimising for throughput, latency, cost, or all three badly at once? What handles routing, auth, observability, and fallbacks around the serving layer? Serving engine KV cache GPU utilisation The name often surfaces in conversations as a badge of technical seriousness. The serious part is not naming it. The serious part is integrating it into a system that survives contact with traffic.

Voice Agent AI voice agent voice AI agent phone agent Agents and workflows Product and vendor terms Economics and operations of AI voice automation customer support operations A synthetic colleague hired to answer the phone with perfect stamina and the occasional inability to notice that a human is furious.
 A voice agent is an AI system that can conduct spoken conversations, usually by combining speech recognition, language models, and text-to-speech in order to handle calls, bookings, support, or outbound interactions.
 It exists because phone-based workflows are expensive, repetitive, unevenly staffed, and still surprisingly central to sales, service, healthcare, logistics, and small business operations.
 It is oversold as if sounding natural were the same as being operationally competent. A smooth voice can still misunderstand intent, mishandle escalation, or create a synthetic version of the same bad call centre experience.
 In practice it usually means handling routine conversations, qualification, scheduling, and after-hours coverage, with humans reserved for edge cases, regulated contexts, and the moments when empathy turns out to require more than latency optimisation.
 "The voice agent could book appointments all evening, though it remained oddly serene when people started describing real problems."
 What percentage of calls can it complete correctly without handoff? How does it detect urgency, compliance risk, or emotional escalation? Are we automating a workflow that is well defined, or merely hiding a broken phone process behind a better accent? Call automation Conversational IVR Speech stack The technical stack matters less than the escalation policy. Voice makes mistakes feel personal very quickly.

Weights model weights parameters Core concepts Model building Infrastructure and deployment parameters hosting openness The dense pile of numbers that turns "just an algorithm" into property, infrastructure, and geopolitical gossip.
 Weights are the learned numerical parameters inside a model. They are what the model ends up using to make predictions and generate outputs after it has been built.
 Because a model is not merely an idea on paper. It becomes operational through the learned values that encode its statistical behaviour.
 By treating weights as synonymous with understanding, openness, or a complete product. Having access to weights matters, but it does not by itself explain data provenance, system quality, tooling, safety controls, or whether the thing belongs anywhere near production.
 In practice it usually means portability, distribution, storage, deployment constraints, adaptation, and endless arguments over whether access to weights counts as openness or merely a different pricing model.
 "They said the weights were available as though that settled the harder questions about trust, licensing, and whether anyone should deploy them unescorted."

Worktree git worktree agent worktree worktree isolation Infrastructure and deployment Agents and workflows git agents workflow isolation A separate checkout where the agent can make a mess without immediately decorating the main desk with it.
 A worktree is a linked working tree attached to the same Git repository, allowing different branches or tasks to be checked out in separate directories at the same time.
 It exists because parallel work is useful and mixing every branch, experiment, dependency state, and agent task into one checkout is how small mistakes become local archaeology.
 It gets treated as a sandbox. It is not one. A worktree isolates files and branches, not credentials, networks, databases, or the agent's ability to run a regrettable command.
 In AI-assisted development, it usually means giving a coding agent or subagent a separate branch and directory so it can edit, test, and report back without colliding with the main checkout or another agent's work.
 "The worktree let three agents work in parallel, which was helpful until everyone remembered they still shared one confused package cache."
 What is isolated by the worktree, and what shared state still leaks across tasks? How will changes be reviewed, merged, kept, or discarded? Do untracked files, environment secrets, and generated artefacts need to be copied or excluded? Git Worktree Subagent Worktree Scratch Checkout

World Model internal world model predictive model of the world Core concepts Model building Cultural terms prediction representation hype The flattering phrase for a system that appears to know enough structure for humans to start projecting philosophy onto it.
 A world model is an internal representation that helps a system predict how states, objects, actions, or events relate to one another over time.
 The term exists because systems that can anticipate structure, consequence, and continuity often perform better than systems that merely react to the next local cue.
 By treating any evidence of internal structure as proof of deep understanding, common sense, or machine comprehension of life itself. The phrase is often used at exactly the point where caution should become more fashionable.
 In practice it usually means a model has learned enough regularity to predict useful consequences, not that it has joined the human condition. It is a technical claim that acquires mystical overtones the moment it leaves the lab.
 "Someone said world model and the meeting immediately upgraded from product review to metaphysical salon."
 What specific predictive behaviour suggests a useful internal model rather than clever pattern matching? How much of the claim comes from experiments, and how much from the romance of the phrase? Does the system actually plan better, or do we simply enjoy the story more? Planning Prediction Internal representation

 Zero data retention is a provider policy or product setting indicating that prompts and outputs are not stored for model improvement or long-term retention beyond limited operational handling.
 Because enterprise buyers, regulated industries, and anyone with a functioning sense of self-preservation want stronger assurances about where sensitive inputs end up after an AI request is made.

 In practice it usually means a procurement checkbox with real value, provided someone also asks about logs, sub-processors, retention exceptions, and whether the promise applies across every feature being sold under the same cheerful brand.

Zero-Shot zero-shot prompting no-example prompting Core concepts Model usage Agents and workflows prompting examples workflow Asking the system to improvise on first contact and congratulating yourself when probability looks like generality.
 Zero-shot refers to asking a model to perform a task without giving task-specific examples in the prompt.
 It is useful to distinguish between tasks a model can handle from instructions alone and tasks that need demonstrations, schemas, retrieval, or tighter scaffolding.
 By treating zero-shot success as proof of broad understanding rather than a description of prompt setup. Many so-called zero-shot wins rely on hidden context, careful wording, or tasks that were never especially demanding.
 In practice it usually means the cheapest first attempt. If the task is routine and the instructions are clear, zero-shot can be enough. If the task is messy, high-stakes, or structurally ambiguous, it often becomes a polite way to under-specify the work.
 "They celebrated the zero-shot result right up to the point where the real workflow arrived with edge cases and an invoice."
 Is the task genuinely being done from instructions alone, or is other hidden scaffolding doing the work? What examples, schemas, or retrieval would reduce failure without much extra cost? Are we calling this zero-shot because it is elegant, or because we have not designed the workflow yet? Prompting Few-shot Task specification
